minor.fix: Count skipped lines in the filters and emit one `[forge] skipped N malformed stream-json lines` to stderr at stream end; same counter in readResultFromFile so 'tokens unknown due to malformed sidecar' is distinguishable from 'no result event'.
minor.test: Pipe a fixture with a truncated final result line through claudeJobStreamFilter; assert a stderr warning is produced while stdout stays clean.

serious.title: Launch runner's set_status is a silent no-op — failed runs stay 'running' forever
serious.detail: The generated tmux runner script defines `set_status() { set_meta_field "status" ""$1""; }`. In bash, `""$1""` concatenates to the bare word `failed` (no JSON quotes), and set_meta_field does `d[key] = json.loads(val_json)` with NO try/except fallback (unlike critique.ts's update_meta, which has one). json.loads('failed') raises, the python process exits non-zero, and `2>/dev/null || true` swallows it — the write silently never happens. Reproduced locally: after `set_status "failed"`, meta.json still reads `{"status": "running"}`. Consequences: (1) every failure path in the run loop (agent non-zero exit, no commits, PR creation failure, quality_failed, quality_check/creating_pr/reviewing/fixing transitions) leaves meta.json status='running'; (2) syncPlanStatus and syncJobState mirror meta.json, so the plan, jobs row, `forge status`, `forge run ls`, and the Workbench all show a failed run as running indefinitely — the exact 'lying status' F0 forbids; (3) `forge wait` never reaches its terminal set on a failed run (tmux stays alive on the `read -p` epilogue) and burns the full 30-minute timeout; (4) `set_meta_field "baseSha" ""$BASE_SHA""`, finalSha, and the unquoted reviewError write at launch.ts:696 are dropped the same way. Only the inline python blocks that set status='done' (PR success path) and the quoted-JSON reviewVerdict writes work. The bug has existed since the orchestrator was created (commit fd4598b carries the same line).
serious.evidence: src/core/launch.ts:403-414: `set_meta_field() { python3 -c "... d[key] = json.loads(val_json) ..." "$1" "$2" 2>/dev/null || true }` then `set_status() { set_meta_field "status" ""$1""; }`. Failure-path calls: launch.ts:455 `set_status "failed"` (agent exit), :473 quality_failed, :525 (no commits) and :621 (PR create failed). Repro executed in this audit: seeded meta `{"status":"running"}`, ran the exact functions, `set_status "failed"` left meta unchanged while `set_meta_field "reviewVerdict" '"approve"'` (properly quoted JSON) succeeded. store.ts:406-408 `const newStatus = meta.status ... if (!newStatus || newStatus === plan.status) return null;` shows all downstream state flows from this field.
serious.fix: Emit valid JSON from set_status: `set_status() { set_meta_field "status" "\"$1\""; }` (and same for baseSha/finalSha/reviewError), or port critique.ts's try/except string fallback into the launch runner's set_meta_field. Add a trap/EXIT handler in the runner that force-writes a terminal status if the script exits while meta is non-terminal.
serious.test: Unit test that renders generateRunnerScript, extracts set_meta_field/set_status, runs them in bash against a seeded meta.json, and asserts status actually transitions to 'failed'/'quality_check' (mirroring tests/critique-runner-rescue.test.ts's harness style).

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code reading and an executed repro of the exact generated bash. (1) launch.ts:414 emits `set_meta_field "status" ""$1""` — in bash `""$1""` concatenates to the bare unquoted word, so python's sys.argv[2] is e.g. `failed`, and launch.ts:409 `d[key] = json.loads(val_json)` raises; launch.ts:411 `2>/dev/null || true` swallows it, so the write silently never happens. Repro: seeded {"status":"running"}, ran the verbatim functions; set_status "failed" left meta unchanged while the quoted-JSON reviewVerdict write succeeded, and `set_meta_field "baseSha" ""$BASE_SHA""` was dropped too. (2) Every failure path uses set_status: launch.ts:455 (agent exit), :473 (quality_failed), :525 (no commits), :621 (PR create failed). The only working status write is the inline python at :608-618 that sets 'done' on PR success — so successes look fine and failures lie. (3) No guard rescues this: launch.ts:831 seeds status:"running"; store.ts:400-430 syncPlanStatus only mirrors meta.json (no dead-tmux reconciliation anywhere — grep confirmed; status.ts:62 only displays a cosmetic [dead] tag). (4) wait.ts:122-130's STALLED guard requires tmux to be dead, but launch.ts:847 appends `; read -p 'Press Enter to close...'` so the session stays alive after a failed runner — wait burns the full timeout and exits 4. (5) tests/gh.test.ts:45 only asserts the literal text `set_status "failed"` exists in the script, pinning nothing about behavior. reviewVerdict writes work because __extract-review prints JSON.stringify output (review-actions.ts:517) and the other writes use explicit '"..."'/"null" literals — matching the claim. Severity BLOCKER is correct under the stated lens: failed runs are permanently shown as 'running' in forge status, run ls, jobs, and the Workbench — silent failure of a core flow / lying status.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as written. Two small additions: (a) intermediate transitions (quality_check :466, creating_pr :592, reviewing :629, and the auto-fix block's fixing :138 / reviewing :179) are also silent no-ops, so in-flight progress never surfaces either; (b) a quality_failed run whose draft PR is still created gets its status overwritten to 'done' by the working inline python at launch.ts:608-618, masking the quality failure entirely — slightly worse than the claim states. Also launch.ts:696's reviewError write (`""reviewer process exited with code $REVIEWER_EXIT""`) is dropped the same way, as claimed.

serious.title: GitHub publish of review findings is best-effort: failures are swallowed and the session still reports 'completed'
serious.detail: In the ad-hoc review worker, publishing findings to GitHub is wrapped in a catch that only writes a line into the run-dir agent.log; the session row is then finalized state='completed' with error=null, meta.json status='completed', and no publishStatus/publishedCount is recorded anywhere. If `publishReviewFindings` throws (auth, API host parse, anchor failure, rate limit), the operator sees a green completed review in the Workbench while zero findings reached the PR — exactly the reported top pain ('PR review pipeline does NOT reliably post findings to GitHub'). The only trace is a single `publish to GitHub failed (best-effort): ...` line buried in the SSE log, which is gone from view once the drawer closes. Same pattern in the comment-fix worker for thread resolution/dispute replies (resolvePublishedFindingThreads 'never fails the worker').
serious.evidence: review-actions.ts:437-450: `// Best-effort publish to GitHub ... a failure logs a warning but never fails the review`; review-actions.ts:575-577: `} catch (e) { args.log(`publish to GitHub failed (best-effort): ${(e as Error).message}`); }`. Finalization at review-actions.ts:459 `const status = exitCode === 0 ? "completed" : "failed"` and :473-480 finalizeSession — exitCode is unaffected by publish failure and metricsPatch carries no publish outcome. comment-fix-actions.ts:1253-1255 same swallow: `log(`GitHub write for finding ${target.id} failed (best-effort): ...`)`.
serious.fix: Record publish outcome as first-class state: write `publishStatus: 'published'|'failed'|'skipped'` + per-finding results into meta.json / the session metrics blob, surface it in the review summary API (ReviewRunSummary) and the Workbench, and consider state='completed_with_warnings' or a non-null error string when publish was requested but failed. Retry transient GitHub errors once.
serious.test: Run runReviewWorker with publishToGitHub=true and a stubbed publishReviewFindings that throws; assert the session metrics/meta.json record a failed publish and the /api review summary exposes it (not a clean 'completed').

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed, with one mechanical correction that makes it worse: publishReviewFindings (src/core/gh-pr-write.ts:365-410) never throws by design ("All calls are best-effort... they never throw", gh-pr-write.ts:11-12). Realistic failures — reconciliation fetch failure (returns {posted:0, skippedPost:true} at :372-377) and review POST failure ({posted:0} at :402-404) — are returned as a PublishResult that the caller DISCARDS (bare `await publishReviewFindings(...)` at review-actions.ts:564). The cited catch (:575-577) is only a backstop for unexpected throws. In all cases exitCode stays 0, the session finalizes state='completed', error=null (review-actions.ts:459, 473-480), meta.json says 'completed', and no publish outcome is persisted anywhere: publishReviewFindings is referenced nowhere else in the repo, ReviewRunSummary/ReviewRunDetail (:607-629) have no publish field, and the Workbench has only the opt-in toggle (web/signals/review.ts:109, ReviewActionBar.tsx:56-64), no outcome display. Only test coverage is the toggle gate (tests/review-actions.test.ts:48-50). Minor overstatements: (a) the warning line is persisted to <runDir>/agent.log (review-actions.ts:192,236), not just the ephemeral SSE stream — but it is never surfaced in status or UI; (b) the comment-fix half is weaker: failed thread-resolves leave ghResolved unset and the bundle uses GitHub isResolved as ground truth (serve.ts:1261-1266), so the still-open thread is indirectly visible on reload, though the worker still reports 'completed' and dispute-reply failures (comment-fix-actions.ts:1250,1254) are invisible. Core claim stands: with the Publish-to-PR opt-in on, zero findings can reach GitHub while forge status and the Workbench show a green completed review — a silent failure of exactly the operator's top-pain flow.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced end to end. (1) Publish call site: review-actions.ts:440-450 calls publishFindingsToGitHub only when opted in; that helper (:544-578) swallows throws into a log line. (2) Read publishReviewFindings fully (gh-pr-write.ts:365-410): it converts all gh failures into a PublishResult (skippedPost/posted=0) and never throws; the caller at review-actions.ts:564 ignores the return value, so failures bypass even the catch. (3) Finalization: exitCode/error are untouched by publish failure; status = exitCode===0 ? 'completed' : 'failed' (:459), finalizeSession writes state='completed', error=null, metricsPatch has no publish data (:473-480). (4) Searched the whole repo for any other surfacing: publishReviewFindings used only here; no publishStatus/publishedCount anywhere; review-history API shapes (:607-629) and Workbench review UI (signals/review.ts, ReviewActionBar.tsx, CommentThread.tsx) expose only the opt-in toggle, never an outcome. (5) Tests pin only shouldPublishToGitHub gating. (6) Comment-fix path verified at comment-fix-actions.ts:749-762, 1177-1257: resolve/reply failures only logged; ghResolved=true persisted only on success; serve.ts:1261-1266 makes GitHub isResolved ground truth so that half is partially visible, as noted in correctedDetail. The bug is real and matches the BLOCKER lens (findings invisible on the PR, green status, top-pain core flow).

serious.title: Killed/OOMed review or comment-fix worker leaves session 'running' forever and permanently blocks that PR via the single-flight guard
serious.detail: runAdHocReview/runCommentFix spawn a detached `forge __review-worker` / `__comment-fix-worker` and rely on the worker itself to finalize its sessions row. If the worker dies without running its finalize path (SIGKILL, OOM, machine reboot, bun binary replaced mid-run), the row stays state='running' indefinitely: (1) the Activity view shows a phantom live session; (2) the single-flight guards (`WHERE purpose='review' AND state='running' AND prNum/repoRoot match`) then reject every future review/fix of that PR with REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT / FIX_IN_FLIGHT — and there is no recovery path: the parent never records the worker pid (upsertSession at review-actions.ts:214-231 passes no pid), there is no kill endpoint for sessions, and no reaper covers these purposes (reconcileExecutionSessions is purpose='execution' keyed to jobs; reconcileCritiqueSessions covers 'critique'/'synthesis'; reapStaleCritiques covers critique-meta; reapStalePlanChats covers plan-chat). The operator's only out is manual sqlite surgery on ~/.forge.
serious.evidence: review-actions.ts:170-186: `SELECT id FROM sessions WHERE purpose='review' AND state='running' ... if (inFlight) throw new CliError("REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT", ...)`. comment-fix-actions.ts:304-318 same FIX_IN_FLIGHT guard. db/writes.ts:212-225 reconcileExecutionSessions: `WHERE sessions.purpose = 'execution' ... AND j.session_id = sessions.id` — never matches ad-hoc review sessions (relatedId null, no job). serve.ts kill action (serve.ts:2535-2567) exists only for plans/tmux, not sessions.
serious.fix: Record `pid` in the session row at spawn (Bun.spawn returns it), add a boot + periodic reaper that marks running review/comment-fix sessions failed when the pid is dead (kill(pid,0)) or older than a generous threshold, and/or make the single-flight guard ignore rows older than N hours. Add a 'mark failed / kill' affordance on session rows in the Workbench.
serious.test: Insert a sessions row purpose='review' state='running' with a dead pid, run the reaper, assert it flips to failed and that runAdHocReview no longer throws REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT.

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed as described with one softening: a dead/SIGKILLed/rebooted `__review-worker` or `__comment-fix-worker` does leave its sessions row state='running' forever (no pid stored — upsertSession at review-actions.ts:214-231 and comment-fix-actions.ts:354-375 omit pid despite writes.ts:59 supporting it; parent-side finalizeSession exists only in the spawn-failure catch at review-actions.ts:264-271), and no reaper covers purposes 'review'/'comment-fix' (reconcileExecutionSessions writes.ts:212-225 joins jobs on purpose='execution'; reconcileCritiqueSessions critique.ts:614 covers 'critique'/'synthesis' only; serve.ts kill action 2535-2567 is plan/tmux-scoped; SSE done-emitter serve.ts:636-656 only reads). The guards at review-actions.ts:170-186 and comment-fix-actions.ts:304-320 have no staleness cutoff, so REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT/FIX_IN_FLIGHT permanently blocks that PR. Correction: the operator's only out is NOT sqlite surgery — `forge session finish --id <sid> --exit-code 1` (src/cli/cmd/session.ts:102-148) is a public CLI verb that finalizes the row and clears the guard; however it is documented as an internal bash-runner helper, is not surfaced in `forge status`/Workbench, and requires manually recovering the session id, so it is an accidental hatch rather than a recovery path. Additional impact: worktrees.ts:487 excludes running sessions' cwd from worktree pruning, so the phantom row also pins worktree cleanup. Minor nit: the comment-fix purpose is 'comment-fix', not 'fix'.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced every cited path. (1) Guards: review-actions.ts:170-186 and comment-fix-actions.ts:304-320 select purely on purpose+state='running'+prNum+repoRoot with no started_at staleness escape and throw REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT/FIX_IN_FLIGHT. (2) No pid: both upsertSession calls omit pid; Bun.spawn result is unref()'d and proc.pid never written back. (3) Reaper coverage: grep across src shows the only session-state sweepers are reconcileExecutionSessions (purpose='execution' joined to jobs — ad-hoc review/fix sessions have relatedId null and no job row, so never matched), reconcileCritiqueSessions (purpose IN ('critique','synthesis')), reapStaleCritiques (critique meta), reapStalePlanChats (in-memory chats). (4) No kill endpoint for sessions: serve.ts kill action targets plans/tmux only; SSE log streamer reads session state but never writes it. (5) Tests (review-actions.test.ts, comment-fix-actions.test.ts) pin no reaping behavior. The only refutation found is the 'sqlite surgery is the only out' claim: `forge session finish` (session.ts) can finalize the stuck row from the CLI, but it is an internal-runner helper not surfaced anywhere as a recovery path. Severity HIGH stands: lying live status plus effectively-permanent blocking of the PR review/fix flow, which is the operator's stated top pain; not BLOCKER because no data is dropped and a manual escape exists.

serious.title: PR #64's 'trust the sidecar result event over the exit code' is implemented only for critique — launch agent, launch reviewer/fixer, ad-hoc review, and comment-fix still trust exit codes
serious.detail: critique.ts's runner has crit_slot_valid which both rescues a non-zero-exit claude run whose sidecar shows a valid terminal result AND force-fails a zero-exit run with no valid result. None of the other stream-json pipelines apply either direction: (1) the launch runner aborts the whole job on AGENT_EXIT != 0 even when agent.stream.jsonl contains a clean result and all work was committed (the exact tee/SIGPIPE class PR #64 fixed); (2) the launch reviewer/fixer branches mark reviewVerdict null / stop auto-fix purely on pipeline exit, even though review.stream.jsonl / fix.stream.jsonl are teed right there — a completed review whose pipeline exits non-zero is discarded; (3) runReviewWorker throws 'reviewer agent exited non-zero' on execFileSync failure without checking the sidecar or whether review-raw.md already holds a complete forge-review block; (4) runCommentFixWorker same. Conversely a claude run that exits 0 with an is_error result event is only caught indirectly (no commits / no fenced block), producing misleading error messages. This directly feeds the 'review pipeline unreliable' pain: real reviews are thrown away because of exit-code noise.
serious.evidence: Critique-only rescue: critique.ts:239-266 crit_slot_valid + :309-334 'non-zero exit but valid result — rescued'. Launch agent: launch.ts:447-457 `AGENT_EXIT=$? ... if [ "$AGENT_EXIT" -ne 0 ]; then ... set_status "failed"; exit "$AGENT_EXIT"` — no sidecar check despite agent.stream.jsonl being teed at agents/index.ts:79. Launch reviewer failure branch: launch.ts:691-697 `log "⚠ Reviewer process failed (exit $REVIEWER_EXIT)"; set_meta_field "reviewVerdict" "null"`. Ad-hoc worker: review-actions.ts:402-417 `catch (e) { ... throw new Error(`reviewer agent exited non-zero ...`) }` (sidecar read only for token metrics, not for rescue). comment-fix-actions.ts:656-671 identical.
serious.fix: Factor the crit_slot_valid logic (last result event, is_error, stop_reason allowlist, complete output check) into a shared helper (bash function emitted into the launch runner; TS function for the workers) and apply it at all five sites: rescue non-zero exits with valid results, force-fail zero exits without them.
serious.test: Extend tests/critique-runner-rescue.test.ts-style harness to the launch runner: simulate reviewer command exiting 1 after writing a valid review.stream.jsonl + complete forge-review block; assert reviewVerdict is extracted, not nulled. Same for runReviewWorker with a stub adapter.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line was re-read and matches. (1) critique.ts:239-266 defines crit_slot_valid and :311-334/:388-394 apply it (rescue + force-fail) — and PR #64's commit message (c3be51c) confirms it was applied only to critic A, critic B, and the synthesizer. (2) launch.ts runs the identical vulnerable pipeline shape under set -uo pipefail (:368): agent at :447 `${agentCmd} 2>&1 | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"` where claudeJobCommand (agents/index.ts:79) is `claude … | tee sidecar | filter`; :452-456 aborts on AGENT_EXIT alone with set_status "failed" and never consults agent.stream.jsonl. (3) Launch reviewer else-branch :691-697 sets reviewVerdict null on REVIEWER_EXIT without attempting __extract-review on review-raw.md; auto-fix re-reviewer (:234-240) and fixer (:245-251) break on exit code alone. (4) review-actions.ts:402-417: on execFileSync failure the sidecar is read only via captureSidecarMetrics for tokens, then it throws 'reviewer agent exited non-zero'; extraction/publish at :426-450 is unreachable, so a complete forge-review block in review-raw.md is discarded and the run is marked failed. (5) comment-fix-actions.ts:656-671 is the identical pattern. I searched for any guard elsewhere: readResultFromFile outside critique.ts is used only for token/cost capture (agents/index.ts:173, improve.ts:632), never to override exit codes; no reconciliation path or test rescues these flows. The reverse direction (exit 0 + is_error result) is also only caught indirectly (launch: 'No commits ahead' at :521-527; workers: 'no fenced forge-review block'), producing misleading errors as claimed. Severity HIGH is right: failures are visible (not silent BLOCKER) but completed reviews/work are discarded due to an exit-code-noise class the repo's own PR #64 history proves occurs in practice, directly feeding the 'review pipeline unreliable' pain and producing lying failure causes.

serious.title: No timeout anywhere in the execution layer — a hung agent/gh/git call runs forever with status 'running'
serious.detail: Nothing in the launch runner script bounds the agent, reviewer, fixer, quality commands, gh calls, or git push; the detached review/comment-fix workers call execFileSync with no `timeout` option; runCritiqueSync and improve.ts use unbounded execSync. If an agent CLI hangs (waiting on credential/auth input, network stall, deadlocked tool), the tmux session and meta.json sit at 'running'/'reviewing' indefinitely and nothing ever notices — there is no watchdog, no last-activity heartbeat on jobs, and the only timeout in the system is the client-side `forge wait --timeout` (default 30m), which merely makes the *waiter* give up (exit 4) without changing job state. The plan-chat path is the sole supervised one (5-min reaper). For an 'overnight fleet' product this means one hung run silently occupies its worktree/branch and shows as live work forever.
serious.evidence: launch.ts:447 `${agentCmd} 2>&1 | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"` — bare invocation, no `timeout(1)` wrapper anywhere in the generated script. review-actions.ts:403-407 `execFileSync("bash", ["-c", bashLine], { stdio: [...], env: ghEnv, cwd: repoRoot })` — no timeout key. improve.ts:474 `execSync(cmd, { stdio: ["ignore", out, err], cwd: ... })`. wait.ts:132-137 timeout only throws CliError TIMEOUT client-side; job state untouched.
serious.fix: Add a per-stage budget: wrap agent/reviewer/fixer commands in the runner with `timeout -k 30 <budget>` (configurable per repo), pass `timeout:` to the workers' execFileSync, and on expiry write a terminal 'timeout' status to meta/sessions so it surfaces in forge status and the Workbench.
serious.test: Generate a runner with a stub 'agent' that sleeps past the budget; run it; assert meta.json reaches a terminal timeout/failed state within budget+grace instead of staying running.

verdict.correctedDetail: Claim confirmed as stated, with one cosmetic correction: the title's "no timeout anywhere in the execution layer" is slightly broad — timeouts DO exist in adjacent modules (worktrees.ts git ops 5s-120s, jira.ts 15-30s, repo.ts context probes up to 300s, gh-pr.ts 20s AbortController on findings-publish REST calls). But every path the detail actually cites is genuinely unbounded: launch.ts:447 bare agent pipe, :536 git push, :594 gh pr create, :666/:157/:213 reviewer/fixer, fixer git commit :171 (the documented 1Password gpgsign hang vector); review-actions.ts:403-407 and comment-fix-actions.ts:657 execFileSync with no timeout key, plus both files' runGhSafe (review-actions.ts:582, comment-fix-actions.ts:940); improve.ts:474 and critique.ts:531 unbounded execSync. No watchdog exists: jobs.state 'timeout' appears in schema IN-lists (db/writes.ts:216,223) but has zero producers; reconcileExecutionSessions only propagates already-terminal job states; wait.ts STALLED (exit 3, :124-130) fires only when tmux is dead — a hung process keeps tmux alive so it never trips; wait.ts TIMEOUT (:132-137) is client-side only and leaves job state at 'running'. The sole supervised path is reapStalePlanChats (plan-chat.ts:1154-1186, 5 min), exactly as the auditor said.
verdict.reasoning: I re-traced every cited path. The generated runner script in launch.ts runs the agent (line 447), quality commands (run_cmd eval, 396-401), git push (536), gh pr create (594), reviewer (666), and fixer (157/171/213 incl. git commit, the known 1Password gpg hang) with no timeout wrapper. The detached review worker (review-actions.ts:403-407) and comment-fix worker (comment-fix-actions.ts:657) call execFileSync without the timeout option, as do their runGhSafe helpers; improve.ts:474 and runCritiqueSync (critique.ts:531) use unbounded execSync. I then searched for compensating guards: nothing ever writes jobs.state='timeout' (the state exists only in IN-list reads in db/writes.ts); reconcileExecutionSessions (writes.ts:212-225) only acts on already-terminal job states; wait.ts stall detection (122-130) requires the tmux session to be dead, which a hung-but-alive agent never triggers, and its timeout (132-137) throws client-side without touching job state; status.ts/ls.ts/dashboard only display tmuxAlive, which reads [alive] for a hung session. The only time-based reaper in the codebase is reapStalePlanChats (plan-chat.ts:1154). So a hung agent/gh/git call leaves meta.json at 'running'/'reviewing' forever with the tmux session showing alive — invisible failure of a core flow, matching HIGH (hang, lying status) under the stated severity lens. The only inaccuracy is the title's "no timeout anywhere", since worktrees/jira/repo/gh-pr modules do bound their own calls; the claim's detail is otherwise precise.

serious.title: Forge-made commits (auto-fix round, comment-fix) don't disable gpg signing — 1Password signing locks the headless loop
serious.detail: Two places where Forge itself runs `git commit` inherit the repo's commit.gpgsign config: the auto-fix block in the launch runner and commitAndPush in the comment-fix worker. With the operator's known setup (gpgsign via 1Password, which locks with the screen — the canonical headless trap in this project's memory), these commits block on the 1Password prompt while the laptop is locked/away: the tmux run hangs at status 'fixing' and the comment-fix worker hangs mid-execFileSync, both indefinitely (compounded by the no-timeouts finding). grep confirms no `--no-gpg-sign`, `commit.gpgsign=false`, or GIT_CONFIG override anywhere in src/. The implementing agent's own commits are also exposed, but Forge's own plumbing commits are squarely Forge's responsibility under 'headless or it doesn't count'.
serious.evidence: launch.ts:170-171 (auto-fix block): `git -C "$WORKTREE" add -A` / `git -C "$WORKTREE" commit -m "fix(review): address reviewer feedback (round $FIX_ROUND)"` — no signing override. comment-fix-actions.ts:1265 `execFileSync("git", ["-C", worktreePath, "commit", "-m", msg], { stdio: ["pipe", "inherit", "inherit"] })`. `grep -rn gpgsign|no-gpg-sign|GIT_CONFIG src/` returns nothing.
serious.fix: Run Forge-authored commits with `git -c commit.gpgsign=false commit --no-gpg-sign ...` (and consider exporting GIT_CONFIG_COUNT overrides into the runner env so agent-made commits in Forge worktrees are also unsigned, or document the per-worktree config Forge writes).
serious.test: In a temp repo with commit.gpgsign=true and gpg.program pointed at a script that blocks, run the comment-fix commitAndPush path; assert it completes without invoking the signer.

verdict.reasoning: Re-traced both cited paths. (1) launch.ts:170-171: the generated bash runner's auto-fix block runs `git -C "$WORKTREE" commit -m ...` with no --no-gpg-sign / -c commit.gpgsign=false, inside a detached tmux session while meta status is "fixing"; the only env exports in the runner are PYTHONUTF8 and LANG (launch.ts:378-379). (2) comment-fix-actions.ts:1259-1267: commitAndPush uses execFileSync("git", [..., "commit", ...]) with no timeout option and no signing override, called (line 745) from runCommentFixWorker, which is spawned as a detached, unref'd Bun.spawn child (lines 390-396) — fully headless. Counter-evidence search came up empty: grep over src/**/*.ts (excluding bundled web/dist) finds zero occurrences of gpgsign, no-gpg-sign, GIT_CONFIG, or any gpg handling; worktree creation in repo.ts:362-373 and worktrees.ts is plain `git worktree add` with no local config that would disable signing, and git worktrees share the parent repo's config, so a repo/global commit.gpgsign=true applies. With the operator's known 1Password gpg setup, both commits block indefinitely when the screen is locked: the tmux run hangs at status "fixing" and the worker hangs mid-execFileSync. HIGH severity is correct under the stated lens (headless-blocking hang). These are Forge's own plumbing commits, squarely within "headless or it doesn't count".
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as stated. One additional wrinkle the auditor missed that makes the auto-fix site slightly worse: the runner script uses `set -uo pipefail` without -e and never checks the commit's exit status, so if gpg fails fast (instead of hanging) the block still logs "✓ Fix committed and pushed" (launch.ts:172-173) and proceeds to re-review a PR diff that does not contain the fixes — a silent-failure variant on top of the hang.

serious.title: Runner death (reboot, tmux kill, OOM) leaves jobs/plans 'running' forever — no reaper exists for jobs, unlike critiques and plan-chats
serious.detail: If the tmux session or the bash runner dies without writing a terminal status (machine reboot, `tmux kill-server`, OOM-killed agent taking the pane down, power loss), meta.json keeps its last in-flight status and nothing ever reconciles it: syncPlanStatus only mirrors meta.json (store.ts:400-430), reconcileExecutionSessions only fixes sessions whose *job row* is already terminal, and the serve boot sweeps cover only critique-meta and plan-chat artifacts — there is no equivalent for runs/jobs. `forge status` does display `tmux: ... [dead]` next to `status: running`, but `forge ls`, `forge run ls`, the jobs table, and the Workbench keep reporting the run as live indefinitely; doLaunch refuses to relaunch (BAD_STATE: not draft/failed/quality_failed), so the only recovery is noticing the contradiction and using the Workbench kill action. `forge wait` detects the dead-tmux case (exit 3 STALLED) but only while someone is actively waiting, and it doesn't repair state either.
serious.evidence: serve.ts:2707-2716 boot sweep: only `reapStaleCritiques(store)` and `reapStalePlanChats(store.forgeDir)` — no job sweep. db/writes.ts:212-224: reconciler requires `j.state IN ('succeeded','failed',...)`, so a job stuck 'running' is untouched. status.ts:50+62 renders `[dead]` but never transitions. launch.ts (cmd) :347-351: `if (task.status !== "draft" && task.status !== "failed" && task.status !== "quality_failed") throw new CliError("BAD_STATE", ... — cannot launch.`
serious.fix: Add a job reaper at serve boot + periodic: for plans in running-family statuses, if `!isTmuxSessionAlive(tmuxSession)` and meta is non-terminal, mergeRunMeta({status:'failed', errorMessage:'runner died'}) + syncJobState (the kill endpoint at serve.ts:2548-2566 already shows the exact recipe). Also let `forge status` offer/perform the same repair.
serious.test: Seed a plan with status 'running' and a nonexistent tmux session; boot startServer (or call the new reaper directly); assert plan/job flip to failed with a 'runner died' summary.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited code point checks out on direct read. serve.ts:2707-2716 boot sweep covers only critique-meta (reapStaleCritiques) and plan-chat artifacts (reapStalePlanChats); the 60s interval at serve.ts:2730-2736 is plan-chat only — no run/job reaper exists. db/writes.ts:212-225 reconcileExecutionSessions only flips sessions whose backing jobs row is ALREADY terminal (j.state IN ('succeeded','failed','timeout','cancelled','hook_denied')), so a job frozen at 'running' is never touched. store.ts:400-430 syncPlanStatus purely mirrors meta.json status with no tmux-liveness input. The generated bash runner in core/launch.ts has no `trap` handler — status only advances via explicit set_status calls — so reboot/OOM/tmux kill leaves meta.json at the last in-flight status forever. Detection is display-only: status.ts:50,62 renders '[dead]' next to status running; ls.ts:50,73 just drops the [tmux] tag; wait.ts:122-130 throws STALLED (exit 3) without repairing state. cli/cmd/launch.ts:347-348 BAD_STATE guard blocks relaunch from any non-draft/failed/quality_failed status. The code itself admits the gap class at launch.ts:856-858 ('no later sync path covers a launch that never started'). Recovery is even narrower than the auditor stated: the TUI dash kill is gated on isTmuxSessionAlive (dashboard.ts:771) so it is unavailable exactly when tmux is dead, and the Workbench kill API (serve.ts:2535-2567) checks KILLABLE_STATUSES = {running, quality_check, creating_pr, fixing} (serve.ts:2027), which excludes the 'reviewing' status the runner writes to meta (and which syncPlanStatus copies into plan.status) — so a run that dies during the review phase has NO in-product recovery path at all. Severity HIGH is correct per the stated lens (lying status across forge ls / run ls / jobs table / Workbench, headless flows like forge wait exit STALLED but nothing self-heals).
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate but slightly understated: (1) the only working recovery, the Workbench kill action, is itself unavailable for runs that die while meta.status='reviewing' because KILLABLE_STATUSES (serve.ts:2027) omits 'reviewing' — yet the runner writes set_status "reviewing" (launch.ts script lines 179/629) and syncPlanStatus copies it into plan.status, leaving such runs with no kill, no relaunch (BAD_STATE), and no reaper; (2) the TUI dashboard kill (dashboard.ts:771) requires isTmuxSessionAlive, so it is disabled precisely in the dead-tmux case, making the Workbench HTTP kill genuinely the sole recovery for the killable statuses.

areas.summary: The data layer is mid-cutover (JSON authoritative, SQLite mirror) and the mirror discipline is inconsistent: some dual-writes are correctly warn-only while three core paths (spec save, improve, plan edit) crash user commands after the JSON commit, and the DB is opened with busy_timeout=0 despite three concurrent writer processes. The worst defect is the runner-only "reviewing" status leaking into Plan.status, which both strands plans permanently out of sync and 500s GET /api/plans for every plan — a plausible root cause of the operator's "review pipeline is unreliable" pain. Concurrency hygiene is the weakest area overall: stale O_EXCL lockfiles are unrecoverable, the bash runner writes meta.json unlocked and non-atomically beneath a TS-side lock that assumes otherwise, and the TUI kill path leaves DB jobs running forever. Migrations themselves are transactional and the backfill is carefully idempotent, but there is no purge/cleanup command (operators are hand-editing both stores, per the .bak-purge files) and zero test coverage for locking, atomic writes, or cross-process contention.

minor.title: Bash set_meta_field writes meta.json unlocked and non-atomically — the 'locked merge' in mergeRunMeta protects nothing, enabling lost updates, torn reads, and silently dropped status transitions
minor.detail: TS-side writers use atomic rename + a `.lock` file (mergeRunMeta), but the runner's set_meta_field/set_status does a python read-modify-write that (a) never takes the lock and (b) truncates meta.json in place (open 'w' + json.dump), so it is neither mutually excluded nor atomic. Consequences: a kill's {status:'failed', errorMessage} merge can be overwritten by an in-flight runner write (the serve.ts comment claiming the lock prevents this is wrong — it only excludes other TS writers); readers can see a torn file — store.readRunMeta returns null (silently skipping a status sync) while mergeRunMeta's bare JSON.parse throws inside the lock, 500ing the kill endpoint; and every python invocation ends `2>/dev/null || true`, so a failed status write (e.g. torn meta from a concurrent writer) is silently swallowed and the plan stays in its previous state with no signal.
minor.evidence: launch.ts:403-412 set_meta_field: `with open('$META_FILE') as f: d = json.load(f) ... with open('$META_FILE', 'w') as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2)" ... 2>/dev/null || true` — no lock, in-place truncate. store.ts:388-397 mergeRunMeta: `withFileLock(\`${p}.lock\`, ...)` with unguarded `JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(p))`. serve.ts:2549-2551 comment: 'Locked merge: the runner's bash set_status writes meta concurrently, so a naive read-spread-write would lose either field' — the runner does not honor that lock.
minor.fix: Have set_meta_field write to a temp file and os.replace() it (atomic), and acquire the same `${meta}.lock` (e.g. mkdir-based lock or a small `forge meta set` CLI helper reusing withFileLock). Guard mergeRunMeta's JSON.parse with a retry/try-catch.
minor.test: Loop a bash set_status writer against concurrent mergeRunMeta calls; assert no merge ever throws and no field update is lost.

minor.title: Migrations run eagerly in every process with no cross-process coordination — racing a newly shipped migration crashes the losing process at startup
minor.detail: ForgeDb runs runMigrations on every construction (serve, every CLI command, every runner-spawned `forge session` helper). The runner reads _migration_history, then applies each unapplied file inside a deferred transaction. Two processes starting concurrently after a new migration ships both see it unapplied; the winner applies it; the loser's `db.exec(sql)` then fails (e.g. 'table X already exists', or SQLITE_BUSY at the write with busy_timeout=0), the transaction throws, and ForgeDb's constructor — hence the whole command or serve startup — crashes. Each individual migration is correctly transactional (SQL + history row commit together), so the DB isn't corrupted, but the crash is user-facing and unexplained, and with serve auto-polling it can recur until timing luck resolves it.
minor.evidence: connection.ts:41 `runMigrations(this.db, ...)` in the constructor. migrations.ts:31-33 reads applied set, then :44-50 `db.transaction(() => { db.exec(sql); db.prepare("INSERT INTO _migration_history ...").run(...) })()` — check-then-act with no lock between the SELECT and the apply; no busy_timeout (connection.ts:38-40) and no IMMEDIATE/EXCLUSIVE begin.
minor.fix: Begin an IMMEDIATE/EXCLUSIVE transaction (or take a file lock on forge.db.migrate.lock) and re-check _migration_history inside it before applying; treat 'already exists'/duplicate-history errors as another-process-won and continue.
minor.test: Spawn two processes that both construct ForgeDb against a DB missing the latest migration; assert both exit 0 and the migration is applied exactly once.

minor.title: recordPlanVersionAdded sets plans.current_version_id unconditionally after INSERT OR IGNORE — UNIQUE(plan_id,version_number) collision with a backfill row leaves a dangling pointer
minor.detail: recordPlanVersionAdded inserts `pv-{plan}-v{n}` with INSERT OR IGNORE, then unconditionally points plans.current_version_id at that id. If a `bf-pv-{plan}-v{n}` row already owns (plan_id, version_number) — possible after the JSON index and DB get out of step (a previously failed live write later healed by `forge migrate`, or a DB restored from one of the manual .bak-purge backups while index.json kept advancing), the insert silently no-ops and current_version_id now references a row that was never created. plans.current_version_id has no FK (schema line `current_version_id TEXT`), so SQLite accepts it. Downstream, recordCritiqueStarted targets that dangling id, so critic_runs/critic_syntheses join nothing and the critique never appears in `forge history` — findings silently invisible. backfill.ts explicitly documents and avoids this same hazard in the opposite direction (it resolves by (plan_id, version_number)), but the live writer doesn't.
minor.evidence: writes.ts:417-433 `INSERT OR IGNORE INTO plan_versions ...` then `db.prepare("UPDATE plans SET current_version_id = ?, updated_at = ? WHERE id = ?").run(planVersionId, ...)` — uses the desired id, not the surviving row's. backfill.ts:290-302 comments the inverse hazard: 'UNIQUE(plan_id, version_number) can block the insert ... Resolve to whatever id actually owns this pair so the downstream FK holds.' history.ts:147-152 joins `plan_versions pv ON pv.id = cr.target_id` — dangling target_id rows vanish.
minor.fix: After the INSERT OR IGNORE, re-select the row owning (plan_id, version_number) — as backfill.ts does — and use that id for current_version_id and tasks.plan_version_id.
minor.test: Pre-insert bf-pv-X-v2 for a plan, call recordPlanVersionAdded(version=2), assert plans.current_version_id resolves to an existing plan_versions row.

minor.title: If recordJobStarted fails, the runner still creates an execution session pointing at a nonexistent jobs row — reconcile can never close it
minor.detail: recordJobStarted failures are warn-only (correct for the cutover), but the runner script already embeds the deterministic job/session ids and calls `forge session start` regardless. The resulting sessions row has related_id = a jobs id that was never inserted. reconcileExecutionSessions — the documented safety net for runners that die before `forge session finish` — joins sessions to jobs, so an orphan session that never gets its finish call stays state='running' forever and pollutes the Activity 'Live' view (phantom running rows, exactly what the sweep exists to prevent). Separately, nextJobRunNumber falls back to 1 on DB error while recordJobStarted recomputes MAX+1, so the script's embedded session id can disagree with jobs.session_id.
minor.evidence: launch.ts:861-867 try/catch warn around recordJobStarted; launch.ts:443-445 script already contains `EXEC_SESSION_ID=...; forge_session_start --id "$EXEC_SESSION_ID" ... --related-id "$JOB_ID"`. writes.ts:212-225 reconcileExecutionSessions: `FROM jobs j ... AND j.session_id = sessions.id` — no jobs row, no sweep. launch.ts:282-295 nextJobRunNumber `catch { return 1; }`.
minor.fix: Make reconcileExecutionSessions also expire 'running' execution sessions whose related_id has no jobs row after a grace period; or have `forge session start` self-heal the jobs row (it has plan id derivable from the deterministic id).
minor.test: Insert an execution session with related_id pointing at no jobs row, run reconcileExecutionSessions with a past timestamp, assert it transitions to failed/expired.

minor.title: readIndex swallows parse errors into an empty index — the next upsertPlan silently rewrites index.json with a single plan, discarding all others
minor.detail: readIndex returns {plans:{}} on any read/parse failure. upsertPlan does read→mutate→write under the lock, so if index.json is ever unreadable (external corruption, manual edit, partial copy during one of the manual purge/restore operations evidenced by ~/.forge/*.bak-purge*), the very next write persists an index containing only the plan being upserted — every other plan record (status, prUrl, worktree, branch, jira links) is silently destroyed. Specs and run dirs survive on disk but become unreachable from all list surfaces. Same pattern in readRepoConfigFile/setRepoConfig for operator settings. atomicWriteJSON makes self-inflicted torn writes unlikely, but the failure mode on any corruption is silent total loss instead of a loud refusal.
minor.evidence: store.ts:299-301 `} catch { return { version: 1, plans: {} }; }` in readIndex; store.ts:312-318 upsertPlan: `const index = this.readIndex(); index.plans[plan.id] = plan; this.writeIndex(index);`. Same for repo config at store.ts:226-233 + 243-249. Manual purge/restore history: ~/.forge/index.json.bak-purge-20260602, index.json.bak-purge2-20260602 exist.
minor.fix: Distinguish 'file absent' (empty index OK) from 'file present but unparseable' (throw / move aside to index.json.corrupt-<ts> and refuse writes until resolved). Optionally rebuild from specs/ + SQLite plans table as a recovery path.
minor.test: Write garbage into index.json, call upsertPlan, assert it throws (or quarantines) rather than persisting a one-plan index.

minor.title: No purge/cleanup command exists (operators hand-edit dual stores), and the locking/atomic-write layer has zero test coverage
minor.detail: The .bak-purge files in ~/.forge show the operator manually purged and backed up both index.json and forge.db — there is no `forge purge`/delete command anywhere in src/, so cleanup requires hand-surgery on two stores that must stay consistent (the dangling current_version_id finding shows how restores diverge them). Also, the audit brief's tests/store-atomic.test.ts does not exist: tests/ has no coverage for atomic-write.ts, file-lock.ts (staleness, contention, timeout), concurrent serve+CLI writes, or SQLITE_BUSY behavior; the db-*.test.ts files cover only single-process happy paths.
minor.evidence: grep -rn "purge" over src/ returns no hits; ~/.forge contains forge.db.bak-purge-20260602, forge.db.bak-purge2-20260602, index.json.bak-purge-20260602, index.json.bak-purge2-20260602. tests/ listing contains no store-atomic.test.ts or file-lock test; db test names (db-dual-write/db-job-versioning/db-migrations/db-backfill) are all single-process happy/idempotency paths.
minor.fix: Ship a `forge plan delete --purge-runs` (or `forge gc`) that deletes JSON, run dirs, and DB rows in one transaction-plus-filesystem sequence with a dry-run; add tests for withFileLock contention/staleness and atomicWriteText crash semantics.
minor.test: New test file exercising two concurrent withFileLock writers (no lost update), a pre-existing dead lock (reclaim), and a purge command round-trip leaving both stores consistent.

serious.title: Runner-only status "reviewing" leaks into Plan.status, permanently strands the plan and 500s GET /api/plans
serious.detail: The bash runner writes meta.json status "reviewing" during the review phase (which lasts minutes). If forge serve or the TUI polls during that window (near-certain with the Workbench open), syncPlanStatus copies it into the JSON index via an unchecked cast — but "reviewing" is not in the PlanStatus union. Consequences: (1) serve's /api/plans sync loop only syncs plans in {running, quality_check, creating_pr, fixing}, so a "reviewing" plan is never synced again — the runner's final "done" (and prUrl) never reaches index.json or the DB jobs row; the job stays 'running' in history forever. (2) statusInfo's switch has no "reviewing" case and no default, so it returns undefined and viewTask throws on info.section; handleApi/Bun.serve fetch have no try/catch, so GET /api/plans returns 500 for ALL plans, permanently, even after the run finishes. (3) KILLABLE_STATUSES excludes it, so the plan can't be killed (409). Only `forge wait` (which syncs unconditionally) accidentally heals it. This is a silent, unrecoverable failure of the core run flow and very plausibly underlies the operator's 'review pipeline is unreliable/hard to use' pain.
serious.evidence: launch.ts:629 `set_status "reviewing"` (also :179). store.ts:406 `const newStatus = meta.status as PlanStatus | undefined;` then :410-419 upserts it; store.ts:84 documents RunMeta.status as `PlanStatus | "reviewing"` while PlanStatus (store.ts:16-25) has no "reviewing". serve.ts:826-834 syncs only `running|quality_check|creating_pr|fixing`. serve.ts:270-330 statusInfo switch cases: running/quality_check/creating_pr/fixing/failed/quality_failed/done/archived/draft — no default, implicit undefined return; serve.ts:448 `const info = statusInfo(task, store);` then `section: info.section` → TypeError. serve.ts:2027 `KILLABLE_STATUSES: Set<PlanStatus> = new Set(["running", "quality_check", "creating_pr", "fixing"])`. handleApi (serve.ts:781+) and Bun.serve fetch (serve.ts:2790-2836) have no try/catch on GET.
serious.fix: Either never persist meta-only statuses into Plan (whitelist meta.status against PlanStatus in syncPlanStatus, mapping "reviewing" to a real status), or add "reviewing" as a first-class PlanStatus with statusInfo case, sync-loop inclusion, and KILLABLE entry. Independently: add a default branch to statusInfo and a try/catch in handleApi returning 500 per-request without breaking the whole list.
serious.test: Write meta.json with status "reviewing" for a running plan, call store.syncPlanStatus then serve's viewTask/GET /api/plans: must return 200; then write meta status "done" and assert the plan reaches done in index.json and jobs.state='succeeded' in SQLite.

verdict.reasoning: Re-traced every step. (a) launch.ts:629/:179 write meta.status="reviewing" via set_status (launch.ts:414) for the duration of the reviewer-agent run (minutes). (b) store.ts syncPlanStatus:400-419 has no guard for "reviewing": the early-return at :401 covers only done/failed/draft/archived, and :406 casts meta.status unchecked into Plan.status, so a Workbench poll during the review window upserts status="reviewing" into index.json (RunMeta.status is explicitly typed PlanStatus | "reviewing" at store.ts:84, while PlanStatus at :16-25 lacks it). (c) serve.ts:826-834 only syncs running|quality_check|creating_pr|fixing, so the plan is never re-synced — the runner's final "done" (launch.ts:701) and prUrl never reach index.json. (d) statusInfo (serve.ts:270-330) has cases for exactly the 9 PlanStatus values and no default; it compiles (param typed PlanStatus) but returns undefined at runtime for "reviewing", and viewTask (serve.ts:448, info.section at :459) throws a TypeError. viewTask is mapped over all plans (serve.ts:839), so GET /api/plans 500s for the entire list, permanently. (e) No healing: KILLABLE_STATUSES (serve.ts:2027) excludes "reviewing" so kill 409s (serve.ts:2538); the TUI dashboard syncStatuses (dashboard.ts:348-356) and getRunningPlans (store.ts:326-335) both exclude "reviewing"; forge status (status.ts) only reads. Only wait.ts:106 syncs unconditionally and would heal it. (f) No test or web-UI code mentions "reviewing"; only db/writes.ts:841 and db/backfill.ts:120 map it, showing the value was known but the JSON-index path serve/TUI read was never guarded. The brief meta.status='done' window after PR creation (launch.ts:611-629) is milliseconds, so a poll landing in the minutes-long reviewing window is near-certain with the Workbench open. Severity BLOCKER fits the lens: silent failure of the core run flow, lying status, plan stranded, and the whole Workbench plan list 500s even after the run succeeds.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate with one immaterial nuance: GET handlers indeed lack try/catch (only POST is wrapped, serve.ts:2029-2040), but Bun.serve's error() hook (serve.ts:2783-2789) converts the viewTask TypeError into a 500 JSON body rather than Bun's default error page — the observed effect (500 for all plans on GET /api/plans, permanently until `forge wait <id>` incidentally re-syncs the plan) is exactly as the auditor described.

serious.title: withFileLock leaves a permanent stale lockfile after a crash — all index writes then fail (and serve busy-waits 5s per attempt)
serious.detail: The advisory lock is an O_EXCL lockfile removed in a finally block. If the process is SIGKILLed, OOMs, or the machine loses power while holding it, the lockfile persists forever; there is no staleness detection (no PID liveness, no mtime expiry). Every subsequent upsertPlan/setRepoConfig/mergeRunMeta throws 'Could not acquire lock' after a 5-second synchronous busy-wait (Atomics.wait blocks serve's single thread). In serve, store.syncPlanStatus(t) at serve.ts:833 is uncaught, so every GET /api/plans 500s after freezing the server for 5s per running plan. Headless runs and quality-gate syncs fail repeatedly until the operator manually deletes ~/.forge/index.json.lock — an undocumented recovery path.
serious.evidence: file-lock.ts:31 `fd = fs.openSync(lockPath, "wx")`; :36-38 `if (Date.now() - start > timeoutMs) throw new Error(\`Could not acquire lock...\`)`; unlink only in finally (:46-51) — nothing reclaims a lock whose owner died. store.ts:312-318 upsertPlan wraps every index write in withFileLock(`${this.indexFile}.lock`). serve.ts:833 `store.syncPlanStatus(t)` has no try/catch in the GET /api/plans handler.
serious.fix: Write the holder PID into the lockfile; on EEXIST, read it and reclaim if the PID is dead or the lock mtime exceeds a threshold (e.g. 30s). Alternatively switch to flock(2)-style locks (auto-released on process death) or move index writes into SQLite.
serious.test: Create indexFile.lock manually (no live owner), call upsertPlan: it should reclaim within the timeout instead of throwing; also assert a live-owner lock is still respected.

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed with one refinement: syncPlanStatus only attempts the index lock when meta.json status diverges from the index (store.ts:408 early-return), so GET /api/plans does not freeze 5s for every running plan unconditionally — only for plans with a pending status transition. But with a stale lock that divergence can never be persisted (upsertPlan at store.ts:419 throws every time), so once any running plan transitions, every subsequent poll freezes ~5s (Atomics.wait in file-lock.ts:14-16 blocks the event loop) and returns a JSON 500 via Bun.serve's error() handler (serve.ts:2783-2789); handleApi GET has no try/catch unlike handleApiPost (serve.ts:2029-2040). Verified no staleness detection (file-lock.ts:23-52 is the entire mechanism — O_EXCL create, unlink only in finally, no PID/mtime check), no cleanup anywhere else in src (boot reapers at serve.ts:2709-2715 cover critique-meta/plan-chat only), no tests for file-lock, and no docs mentioning ~/.forge/index.json.lock or recovery. The trigger window is narrow (ms-scale read-modify-write), but Ctrl-C/SIGINT mid-critical-section also skips finally in Bun, not just SIGKILL/OOM/power loss. Once triggered the state is permanent and the error message gives no recovery hint.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced every cited line. file-lock.ts:31 creates the lock with "wx", :36-38 throws after 5000ms of Atomics.wait busy-polling, :46-51 unlinks only in finally — there is no PID liveness or mtime expiry, and a repo-wide grep confirms no other code path ever removes a stale lock (serve's boot reapers handle critique-meta and plan-chat artifacts only). store.ts:312-318 wraps every index write (upsertPlan), :243-249 setRepoConfig, :388-397 mergeRunMeta in withFileLock; indexFile is ~/.forge/index.json (store.ts:201-205). serve.ts:833 calls store.syncPlanStatus(t) inside GET /api/plans with no try/catch; handleApi (serve.ts:781) has no wrapper catch (only POST does, serve.ts:2029-2040), so the throw reaches Bun.serve's error() at serve.ts:2783 and returns a 500 after the synchronous 5s stall. The only refinements: the stall fires per divergent-status plan rather than per running plan (store.ts:408 early-return), and the crash-while-holding-lock window is small — but once it happens the failure is permanent, repeated, and the recovery (delete the lock file) is undocumented anywhere in README/docs. No tests pin file-lock behavior. The bug is real as described; HIGH is the right severity (unrecoverable state, repeated server stalls, headless flows failing), not BLOCKER since data already written is not lost and the failure is loud (errors/500s) rather than silent.

serious.title: forge.db opened with busy_timeout=0 — concurrent serve + CLI + runner session-helpers hit SQLITE_BUSY and silently drop writes
serious.detail: connection.ts sets WAL/foreign_keys/synchronous but never busy_timeout; verified empirically that Bun 1.3.13's bun:sqlite default is 0. At least three independent processes write the same DB concurrently: forge serve (syncJobState, reconcile sweeps), CLI commands, and the runner's `forge session start/finish` subprocesses spawned around every agent/review/fix invocation. With timeout 0, any overlapping write transaction throws SQLITE_BUSY immediately. The session helper is documented to swallow errors (exit 0, stderr to session-helper.log) and syncJobState/recordJobStarted are warn-only — so token/cost/session rows and job-state transitions are silently lost, diverging the DB from JSON with no surface in `forge status` or the Workbench. Where writes are NOT wrapped (see data-unwrapped-dual-writes), the same BUSY error instead crashes user-facing commands.
serious.evidence: connection.ts:38-40 sets only `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`, `foreign_keys=ON`, `synchronous=NORMAL` — no busy_timeout (verified: `new Database(...)` then `PRAGMA busy_timeout` → {timeout:0} on Bun 1.3.13). launch.ts:110-113 runner helpers: `bun "$FORGE_BIN" session start "$@" >/dev/null 2>>"$SESSION_HELPER_LOG" || true`. session.ts:22-24 'Failures are logged but never exit non-zero'. store.ts:423-428 syncJobState failure is a stderr warn only.
serious.fix: Add `this.db.exec("PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000")` in the ForgeDb constructor; optionally retry once on SQLITE_BUSY in the dual-write helpers and surface repeated failures as a visible health flag in /api/health.
serious.test: Open two ForgeDb handles on one file, hold an IMMEDIATE transaction in one, run upsertSession via the other: with the fix it should succeed within the timeout instead of throwing SQLITE_BUSY.

verdict.reasoning: Every element of the claim checks out against the code and an empirical repro. (1) src/core/db/connection.ts:37-41 is the sole Database construction in src and sets only WAL/foreign_keys/synchronous; grep confirms no busy_timeout/busyTimeout/SQLITE_BUSY handling anywhere in src or test. (2) Reproduced on Bun 1.3.13: PRAGMA busy_timeout defaults to {timeout:0}, and a second connection's INSERT while another holds a write transaction throws SQLITE_BUSY immediately even in WAL. (3) The multi-writer premise is real: long-lived `forge serve` writes via reconcileExecutionSessions/reconcileCritiqueSessions/syncJobState on timers (src/cli/cmd/serve.ts:1070,1110,2558, setInterval at 728/733/741); every CLI invocation opens its own connection (src/cli/main.ts:132); and the runner spawns `bun "$FORGE_BIN" session start/finish ... || true` subprocesses (src/core/launch.ts forgeSessionHelperShell). Write paths use db.transaction() (src/core/db/writes.ts:340,414,457,553,601), giving real write-lock overlap windows. (4) The silent-swallow paths are exactly as cited: session.ts:43-56 catches all errors and exits 0; store.ts:423-428 syncJobState is warn-only; launch.ts:861-867 recordJobStarted is warn-only. So a BUSY collision silently drops sessions/job rows with no surface in forge status or the Workbench. Bounding note: comments (launch.ts:858-861) state meta.json/JSON is still the live source of truth during the dual-write cutover, so the loss diverges the DB mirror (sessions/token/cost, Usage dashboard) rather than killing the core JSON-driven flow — consistent with HIGH rather than BLOCKER. Severity HIGH as stated is correct; the fix is a one-line PRAGMA busy_timeout in connection.ts.

serious.title: recordPlanCreated / recordPlanVersionAdded are not error-guarded — a DB hiccup fails commands AFTER the JSON commit, causing duplicate plans and lying improve results
serious.detail: The dual-write convention elsewhere (critique.ts, launch.ts, store.syncPlanStatus, serve kill) wraps SQLite mirror writes in try/catch with a warning, because JSON is authoritative during cutover. Three call sites skip the guard: saveSpec (spec.ts:299), the auto-improver (improve.ts:697), and plan edits (plan-edit.ts:263). If the SQLite write throws (e.g. SQLITE_BUSY with timeout 0, or a wedged WAL), the exception fires after the spec file and index.json were already written: (a) `forge spec save` reports failure though the plan exists — a retry mints a duplicate plan with a new id; (b) the improver throw is caught by saveSpec's wrapper and reported as mode 'skipped'/IMPROVE_FAILED although the improved spec v2 was already applied to disk and the index — the Workbench failure chip invites a retry that creates a spurious v3; (c) plan-edit HTTP requests 500 though the edit persisted. In all three the DB silently misses the version row, so `forge history` (pure DB read) under-reports.
serious.evidence: spec.ts:298-299 `store.upsertPlan(task); recordPlanCreated(store.db.db, task, fullSpec);` — no try/catch. improve.ts:694-697 `store.writeSpec(...); store.upsertPlan(updatedTask); recordPlanVersionAdded(...)` — no try/catch, and spec.ts:306-319 converts the throw into `mode: "skipped", error: IMPROVE_FAILED` although the spec was applied. plan-edit.ts:260-266 same pattern. Contrast critique.ts:464-471 and launch.ts:861-867 which explicitly catch and warn ('DB failure is warned, not fatal — the ... json is the live source of truth').
serious.fix: Wrap all three in the same try/catch-warn pattern; record the miss (e.g. a dirty flag or retry queue) so `forge migrate from-json` or a read-path self-heal can reconcile later. For improve, set lastImproveError only when the spec genuinely wasn't applied.
serious.test: Monkeypatch store.db.db.prepare to throw inside recordPlanVersionAdded; assert saveSpec still returns success with the plan saved once, and improve result reports applied=true with a DB-sync warning.

verdict.reasoning: Re-traced all three cited sites. spec.ts:296-299 writes spec file + index.json (upsertPlan -> withFileLock -> writeIndex, store.ts:312-318) before calling recordPlanCreated with no try/catch; a throw fails saveSpec after JSON state is committed, and a retry mints a new id (generateId = slug + Date.now().toString(36), store.ts:432-439) creating a duplicate plan. improve.ts:694-697 applies the improved spec to disk and the index before the unguarded recordPlanVersionAdded; the throw is converted to mode "skipped"/IMPROVE_FAILED by both catch wrappers (saveSpec spec.ts:306-319 and improveSpec spec.ts:461-475), and persistImproveOutcome sets lastImproveError, which drives the Workbench failure chip + retry buttons (spec.ts:327-345) — retry would produce a spurious v3. plan-edit.ts:260-266 has the same pattern; in acceptPendingPlanEdit a throw additionally skips deletePendingPlanEdit (line 289), leaving a pending edit that 409s as STALE_EDIT on the next accept. The contrast convention is real and explicit: critique.ts:462-471, src/core/launch.ts:861-867, store.ts syncPlanStatus:421-428, and spec.ts:562-567 (recordPlanArchived) all wrap mirror writes in try/catch with a warn, with comments stating JSON is the live source of truth during cutover. The failure mode is plausible: connection.ts:37-40 sets WAL but no busy_timeout (bun:sqlite default 0), and multiple processes write the DB, so SQLITE_BUSY throws on contention. forge history is a pure DB read (history.ts:39 "SELECT id FROM plans", :47 buildPlanHistory(store.db.db,...)), so missed rows under-report (though recordPlanVersionAdded's ensurePlanAndSyntheticTask self-heal at writes.ts:414-415 can backfill the plan row on a later successful write). Severity HIGH is appropriate: lying status and duplicate-state-on-retry, conditional on DB contention; not BLOCKER since no findings are dropped and the failure is noisy at the moment it occurs.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as stated. Minor additions: (1) the improve path also skips markCritiqueViewed (improve.ts:700) when the unguarded write throws; (2) in acceptPendingPlanEdit the throw also leaves the pending edit undeleted, so the next accept attempt fails with STALE_EDIT (409) until the operator rejects it; (3) recordPlanVersionAdded self-heals missing plan/task rows on a later successful call, slightly softening the permanent-DB-gap consequence for recordPlanCreated but not for the missed version row.

serious.title: TUI dash kill updates only index.json — meta.json and the SQLite jobs row stay 'running' forever
serious.detail: The dash kill action kills tmux and upserts the plan as failed, but unlike the serve kill handler it neither merges meta.json (status/errorMessage) nor calls syncJobState. Because the plan's JSON status is now terminal, syncPlanStatus early-returns forever, so no later read path heals the DB: the jobs row stays state='running' with no finished_at, history shows 'rN started' with no completion, reconcileExecutionSessions (which derives session state from jobs) keeps the execution session alive in the Activity 'Live' filter indefinitely, and failureMessage finds no meta.errorMessage so the Workbench failure card has no reason. Direct 'no silent failure' violation: a deliberate operator action produces a permanently lying DB state.
serious.evidence: dash.ts:79-91 kill case: `execSync(\`tmux kill-session...\`)` then `store.upsertPlan({ ...action.task, status: "failed", completedAt: ... })` — nothing else. Compare serve.ts:2549-2566: `store.mergeRunMeta(planId, { errorMessage: "Killed by user", status: "failed" })` plus `syncJobState(...)` with the comment 'syncPlanStatus early-returns once a plan is terminal, so without this the killed run stays `running` in DB surfaces ... indefinitely'. store.ts:400-402 terminal early-return confirms.
serious.fix: Extract the serve kill body (mergeRunMeta + upsertPlan + syncJobState) into a shared killPlan(store, plan) helper and call it from dash.ts.
serious.test: Launch a fake running plan with a jobs row, invoke the dash kill action, assert jobs.state='failed' with finished_at set and meta.json status='failed'.

verdict.reasoning: Traced the full path; the bug is real and the cited evidence is accurate. (1) dash.ts:79-93 ("K" key, wired at tui/dashboard.ts:225-227): the kill case runs `tmux kill-session` then only `store.upsertPlan({...action.task, status: "failed", completedAt: ...})` — no mergeRunMeta, no syncJobState. (2) The serve.ts kill handler (serve.ts:2534-2566) does all three and its own comment documents exactly why: "syncPlanStatus early-returns once a plan is terminal, so without this the killed run stays `running` in DB surfaces ... indefinitely." (3) store.ts:400-402 confirms the early-return: `if (plan.status === "done" || plan.status === "failed" || ...) return null;` — and syncPlanStatus is the ONLY read-path caller of syncJobState (callers: store.ts:424, serve.ts:2558 only), so once the plan JSON is terminal no later read heals the DB. (4) The jobs row is created with state='running' at launch (writes.ts:490-494 INSERT ... 'running', launch.ts:856-866), and the runner script has no shell trap (grep for "trap" in launch.ts: empty), so tmux kill terminates run.sh before any `set_status` writes a terminal status to meta.json. (5) reconcileExecutionSessions (writes.ts:212-226) only flips sessions whose backing jobs row is in a terminal state ("AND j.state IN ('succeeded','failed',...)"), so the execution session also stays state='running' forever in the Activity Live filter. No other sweeper exists (searched for has-session/orphan/stale liveness checks against jobs — none). Severity HIGH is correct: a deliberate operator kill produces a permanently lying jobs/sessions DB state, matching the "lying status" HIGH criterion. One minor overstatement only, noted in correctedDetail.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate with one minor overstatement: "the Workbench failure card has no reason" is not strictly true. failureMessage (serve.ts:332-344) does miss meta.errorMessage (never written on dash kill), but it falls back to `store.tailLog(task.id, 1)` — so the card typically shows the last agent log line as the "reason" rather than being empty. That arguably makes it worse (a misleading reason instead of "Killed by user"), but it is not blank. Also, meta.json does not literally "stay 'running'" in all cases — it stays at whatever non-terminal status the runner last wrote (running/quality_check/creating_pr/etc.), since the runner script has no trap to write a terminal status on tmux kill. The core defect stands exactly as described: dash.ts kill omits mergeRunMeta + syncJobState, and store.ts:400-402's terminal early-return guarantees the jobs row stays state='running' with NULL finished_at forever, keeping the execution session 'running' in the Activity Live filter via the reconcileExecutionSessions join condition (writes.ts:218-226).

serious.title: Runner writes status 'done' before the review/auto-fix phase — a poll in that window terminally latches the plan as done while the fixer is still pushing commits
serious.detail: Immediately after `gh pr create` succeeds, the runner writes meta.json status='done' (plus prUrl/endedAt/durationMs), then lines later flips to 'reviewing' and runs the reviewer and up to N auto-fix rounds (which commit and push to the PR branch), finally writing 'done' again. If serve/dash/wait polls in the done→reviewing window, syncPlanStatus stamps the plan done + completedAt and syncJobState marks the jobs row succeeded with finished_at; the terminal guard (store.ts:400-402) then blocks all further sync, so a subsequent reviewer failure, fixer failure, or kill never reaches `forge status`/Workbench — the operator sees 'PR opened / done' and may merge while the fixer is still force-feeding commits. The window is small per run but the pipeline runs constantly and serve polls every plan on every /api/plans request.
serious.evidence: launch.ts:608-618 python block: `d['status'] = 'done'; d['prUrl'] = ...` right after PR creation; launch.ts:629 `set_status "reviewing"`; launch.ts:701 final `set_status "done"` after the auto-fix block. store.ts:400-402 `if (plan.status === "done" || ... ) return null;` — once latched, no further meta transitions sync. writes.ts:809-827 syncJobState sets jobs.state='succeeded' and finished_at on the same observation.
serious.fix: Don't write 'done' at PR creation; keep status 'creating_pr' (record prUrl/prNumber as separate fields, which set_meta_field already supports) and only write the terminal status once at the end of the script — including the reviewer/auto-fix outcome.
serious.test: Drive a fake runner that writes done→reviewing→failed into meta.json with a sync poll between each write; assert the plan's final index status is failed, not done.

verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as stated. One refinement on window size and one on poll scope: (1) the done->reviewing window is the gap between two python3 spawns — the inline block at launch.ts:608-618 writes status='done' (plus endedAt/durationMs, so syncJobState gets a real finished_at), and set_status "reviewing" at launch.ts:629 goes through set_meta_field (launch.ts:403-414), which spawns another python3; only an `if` test and two log calls sit between, so the window is roughly tens of milliseconds per run. (2) /api/plans does not sync "every plan" — serve.ts:826-833 syncs only plans whose index status is running/quality_check/creating_pr/fixing — but the plan's index status during this window IS 'creating_pr' (set at launch.ts:592 and synced by earlier polls), so it is in the sync set; wait.ts:106 also calls syncPlanStatus unconditionally every poll, and forge wait --until done returns the moment cur.status==='done' (wait.ts:108-118), letting automation merge while the fixer later commits and pushes (launch.ts:167-173). Once latched: store.ts:400-402 returns null for terminal plans forever; the only other index-status writer is the kill handler, which 409s because KILLABLE_STATUSES (serve.ts:2027) excludes 'done'; subsequent reviewer/fixer failures only write reviewError/reviewVerdict into meta.json (launch.ts:680-696, 221-249) and never re-touch plan.status or the jobs row, which syncJobState (writes.ts:798-828) already stamped state='succeeded' + finished_at on the latching observation. Note the early 'done' write is not dead code — it is the intended terminal status for the PR_NUMBER-extraction-failure path (launch.ts:626 requires both PR_URL and PR_NUMBER to enter review) — so the fix must preserve that fallback while not exposing 'done' transiently on the happy path (e.g., write 'creating_pr'->'reviewing' atomically and only write 'done' once, or have the review-eligibility check happen before the meta write).
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced every cited line. (a) launch.ts:608-618: verbatim `d['status'] = 'done'` written to meta.json immediately after gh pr create succeeds, with prUrl/endedAt/durationMs. (b) launch.ts:629 `set_status "reviewing"` and launch.ts:701 final `set_status "done"` after ${autoFixBash} — both confirmed; auto-fix rounds really do `git add -A; git commit; git push` to the PR branch (launch.ts:170-173). (c) store.ts:400-402: `if (plan.status === "done" || ...) return null;` — terminal guard confirmed; syncPlanStatus is the ONLY mechanism that propagates meta.json status to the plan index (callers: serve.ts:833, wait.ts:106, dashboard.ts:355; grep found no others). (d) A poll in the window latches: serve.ts /api/plans syncs plans in 'creating_pr' (the index status at that moment) on every request; wait.ts syncs unconditionally per poll; syncPlanStatus then stamps status='done' + completedAt (store.ts:410-419) and syncJobState (writes.ts:798-828) sets jobs.state='succeeded', finished_at=meta.endedAt, tasks.state='completed', plans.stage='completed'. (e) No recovery path: kill handler (serve.ts:2535-2566) rejects with 409 because KILLABLE_STATUSES (serve.ts:2027) = {running, quality_check, creating_pr, fixing}; later meta transitions ('reviewing', 'fixing', reviewError on fixer/re-reviewer failure at launch.ts:221-249) never reach the index or jobs row. (f) forge status prints task.status from the index (status.ts:57-59) and viewTask uses task.status (serve.ts:447+), so the latched 'done' is what the operator sees; forge wait --until done exits successfully in the window. The auditor's caveats are honest (small window, constant polling); I found no guard, lock, or test that prevents the race — set_meta_field is itself an unlocked python read-modify-write, and store.mergeRunMeta's lock is not used by the runner script. HIGH is the right severity under the mission lens (lying status / premature terminal state during an active core flow), not BLOCKER, since review findings themselves are still written to disk and the PR exists.

areas.summary: The CLI's command shells are mostly disciplined (CliError envelope, exit-code map, good hints on unknown ids), but the surface fails the F0 bar in two core ways: 'forge status'/'forge ls' never reconcile index.json with the runner's meta.json, so a CLI-only operator sees stale 'running' states and never sees the recorded failure reason (errorMessage/reviewVerdict are JSON-only); and the universal parseArgs strict:false plus first-position-only help means typo'd flags and trailing --help are silently swallowed — including on mutating commands like launch. Separately, running `bun test` provably writes fixture plans into the operator's real ~/.forge because Bun's os.homedir() ignores the tests' HOME redirect (the production index already contains 16+ /tmp/repo junk plans). Several dead ends round it out: hints naming nonexistent 'forge resume'/'forge kill', dash kill leaving meta/jobs rows running forever, logs -f hanging after log rotation, and plan update blocking on stdin with no TTY guard.

minor.title: Error hints direct the operator to commands that don't exist: 'forge resume' and 'forge kill'
minor.detail: launch.ts BAD_STATE hint says 'Use `forge resume` for partial-failure recovery', and worktree.ts WORKTREE_IN_USE hint says 'Kill the session first (forge kill / or via the dashboard)'. Neither 'resume' nor 'kill' exists in main.ts's dispatch switch (and serve.ts explicitly returns 501 'forge resume is not wired in this version'). An operator with a stuck task in state 'running' hits a hard dead end: launch refuses with BAD_STATE, the suggested recovery command is UNKNOWN_CMD, and the only real escape paths (Workbench kill button, tmux kill-session + manual index edit) are undocumented in the CLI.
minor.evidence: launch.ts:349-351 `hint: "Use \`forge resume\` for partial-failure recovery."`; worktree.ts:145 `hint: "Kill the session first (forge kill / or via the dashboard), then retry."`; main.ts:140-214 switch has no 'resume' or 'kill' case; serve.ts:2526-2532 returns 501 NOT_IMPLEMENTED for resume.
minor.fix: Either implement a minimal `forge kill <id>` (tmux kill-session + mergeRunMeta failed + syncJobState, mirroring serve.ts:2535-2567) and fix the hints, or rewrite both hints to real actions ('tmux kill-session -t <session>' / 'kill it from the Workbench, then re-launch').
minor.test: Grep-based test asserting every `forge <verb>` string appearing in CliError hints matches a case in main.ts's dispatch.

minor.title: Dash TUI kill marks the plan failed but leaves meta.json and the SQLite jobs row in 'running' forever
minor.detail: dash.ts's kill action kills tmux and upserts the plan to status 'failed', but unlike the serve.ts kill endpoint it neither writes meta.json (mergeRunMeta) nor calls syncJobState. Because syncPlanStatus early-returns for terminal plans (store.ts:401), the stale meta/job rows are never reconciled afterwards: `forge status --json` shows run.status 'running' for a failed task, no errorMessage is recorded, and `forge run ls` / history / the Runs tab show the job as running indefinitely — the exact stuck state serve.ts's own comment warns about.
minor.evidence: dash.ts:79-95 kill case: `execSync(\`tmux kill-session...\`); store.upsertPlan({ ...action.task, status: "failed", completedAt: ... })` — no mergeRunMeta/syncJobState. serve.ts:2551-2561 does `store.mergeRunMeta(planId, { errorMessage: "Killed by user", status: "failed" }); ... syncJobState(...)` with comment 'without this the killed run stays `running` in DB surfaces (`forge run ls`, Runs tab, history) indefinitely.' store.ts:400-402 syncPlanStatus early-returns when plan.status === "failed".
minor.fix: Extract serve.ts's kill body into a shared killPlan(store, task) helper and call it from both serve and dash.
minor.test: Unit test killPlan: seed running plan + meta + job row, kill, assert meta.status=failed with errorMessage and jobs.state terminal.

minor.title: forge spec ls outside a git repo silently lists every repo's drafts instead of erroring like forge ls
minor.detail: spec.ts runLs treats a failed repo detection as 'no filter': detectRepo(cwd) returning null makes repoRoot undefined, and getPlans(undefined) returns all plans across all repos. Verified from /tmp: `forge ls` correctly errors NOT_A_REPO (exit 2) but `forge spec ls` exits 0 and printed drafts from other repos (including the /tmp/repo test-fixture junk). Help text says 'List saved specs in the current repo', so the output silently lies about its scope — an operator can act on a draft belonging to a different repo without realizing it.
minor.evidence: spec.ts:726-729 `const detected = detectRepo(process.cwd()); const repoRoot = detected?.root; const allTasks = store.getPlans(repoRoot);` vs ls.ts:99-105 which throws NOT_A_REPO. Live: from /tmp, `forge ls` → 'error: Not in a git repository.' exit 2; `forge spec ls` → exit 0 listing task-defonly-001 etc. HELP at spec.ts:73-75: 'List saved specs in the current repo.'
minor.fix: Mirror ls.ts: throw NOT_A_REPO with hint to pass --all/--repo, and add those flags to spec ls.
minor.test: Run spec ls with cwd in a non-repo temp dir; assert exit 2 NOT_A_REPO (or that output is explicitly labeled all-repos).

minor.title: forge plan update/set-question content fallback reads stdin unconditionally — interactive invocation hangs with no prompt
