minor.detail: plan.ts readContent falls through to readStdin() when neither --content nor --from-file nor '-' is given. Unlike spec save (which throws NO_INPUT when process.stdin.isTTY), there is no TTY guard, so `forge plan update <id> --section goals` typed at a terminal silently blocks forever waiting for Ctrl-D, with no prompt or error. In a CI/headless context with an attached-but-silent stdin it hangs the pipeline — the canonical 'blocks on interactive input' trap F0 forbids.
minor.evidence: plan.ts:67-75 `if (positionals.includes("-")) return await readStdin(); return await readStdin();` — unconditional fallback. Contrast spec.ts:372-377 `if (process.stdin.isTTY) { throw new CliError("NO_INPUT", ...) }`.
minor.fix: Copy the spec save guard: if no --content/--from-file and stdin isTTY, throw CliError NO_INPUT with hint 'pass --content, --from-file, or pipe content'.
minor.test: Invoke runUpdate with a mocked TTY stdin and assert it throws NO_INPUT instead of awaiting stdin.

minor.title: ForgeStore/SQLite open and migrations run before the try block — a corrupt forge.db yields a raw stack trace and breaks --json error contract
minor.detail: main.ts constructs ForgeStore and force-opens the DB (`void store.db`, running migrations) at lines 132-136, but the CliError→envelope catch only wraps the switch starting at line 139. bin/forge.ts is a bare top-level `await run(...)` with no catch. Any failure opening ~/.forge/forge.db (corruption, lock, failed migration) therefore escapes as an unhandled rejection: Bun prints a raw stack trace, --json consumers (cc-plugin and scripts that parse the documented {ok:false,...} envelope) get non-JSON on stdout/stderr, and the exit code is Bun's default rather than the documented 3.
minor.evidence: main.ts:132-137 `const store = new ForgeStore(); ... void store.db; const json = isJsonRequested(rest);` precede `try {` at main.ts:139. bin/forge.ts:8 `await run(process.argv.slice(2));` with no catch. output.ts:10-15 documents exit code 3 for runtime failure.
minor.fix: Move store construction + `void store.db` inside the try (json detection can stay above), or wrap them in their own try that emits CliError("DB_OPEN_FAILED", ..., {exitCode: 3, hint: 'check ~/.forge/forge.db'}).
minor.test: Point ForgeStore at a deliberately corrupted forge.db (temp dir) and assert the CLI exits 3 with an {ok:false} JSON envelope under --json.

minor.title: forge logs -f parks forever after the log file is rotated/removed — watcher closes but the process never exits
minor.detail: The follow path parks on `await new Promise<void>(() => {})` and relies solely on SIGINT to exit. When the watched file is removed or rotated (run dir cleanup, worktree removal), the watch callback's statSync throws, the code closes the watcher ('file rotated or removed — stop') — and then nothing: the parked promise never resolves, so the process sits silently forever producing no output. In a headless script (`forge logs <id> -f` feeding a monitor), that is an indefinite hang with no error and no exit code. There is also no exit when the run reaches a terminal state, so scripted follows never terminate even on success.
minor.evidence: logs.ts:78-93: `const watcher = fs.watch(logFile, () => { try { stream(...) } catch { // file rotated or removed — stop\n watcher.close(); } }); ... await new Promise<void>(() => {});` — the comment says 'stop' but only the watcher stops; the promise at line 93 never settles.
minor.fix: Hold a resolve fn for the parked promise; call it (and process.exit cleanly with a 'log file removed' notice) in the catch branch. Optionally also poll plan/meta status each few seconds and exit 0 when the run is terminal and no new bytes arrive.
minor.test: Start logs -f on a temp file in a child process, delete the file, assert the child exits within a bounded time with a message rather than hanging.

minor.title: forge ls renders archived plans with the running spinner icon and includes them by default
minor.detail: statusIcon() has cases for done/failed/quality_failed/draft and defaults everything else to '⟳' (the in-progress glyph). 'archived' (a first-class PlanStatus) hits the default, so archived specs render exactly like running work. Verified live: `forge ls` printed '⟳ test-workbench-submit-click-test-moz2i7ps  archived ...'. An operator scanning for active jobs sees phantom in-flight tasks. The same default would also apply to the undeclared 'reviewing' status that syncPlanStatus can copy from meta.json.
minor.evidence: ls.ts:54-66 statusIcon switch — no 'archived' case, `default: return "⟳";`. Live output: '⟳ test-workbench-submit-click-test-moz2i7ps  archived  forge/test-workbench-planner-chat-smoke-test'. store.ts:16-25 PlanStatus includes "archived".
minor.fix: Add an 'archived' case (e.g. '▪' or '○') and consider excluding archived from default forge ls (matching spec ls), behind --all.
minor.test: Snapshot test of humanFormat with one plan per PlanStatus asserting distinct icons and that archived is visually distinct from running.

minor.title: Numeric/state flag values are not validated: status --tail NaN, wait --until typos, ls --status typos silently do the wrong thing
minor.detail: status.ts parses --tail with parseInt and no NaN check — `forge status <id> --tail abc` silently drops the log tail (verified: output ended at 'pr: (none)', exit 0). wait.ts parseUntil casts any token to PlanStatus without validation, so `--until doen` (typo) can never be satisfied and the command burns the full 30m timeout (exit 4) or stalls. ls --status accepts unknown statuses and just prints '(no tasks)'. None of these tell the user their input was meaningless.
minor.evidence: status.ts:48 `const tailN = values.tail ? Number.parseInt(values.tail as string, 10) : ...` then line 51 `tailN > 0` (NaN > 0 → false, no error). wait.ts:67-74 `out.statuses.add(p as PlanStatus);` — no membership check against PlanStatus. Verified `--tail abc` exit 0 with no tail and no warning.
minor.fix: Throw CliError BAD_ARG when --tail isn't a non-negative integer; validate --until/--status tokens against the PlanStatus union (plus 'pr_ready') and error listing the valid set.
minor.test: forge wait <id> --until doen should exit 1 BAD_ARG immediately, not exit 4 after the timeout.

serious.title: forge status and forge ls report stale plan status; they never reconcile with the runner's meta.json
serious.detail: The bash runner only writes status transitions to ~/.forge/runs/<id>/meta.json ('Status is written to ... meta.json by the bash script'). The index.json Plan.status that 'forge status' and 'forge ls' print is only reconciled by store.syncPlanStatus(), which is called from forge wait, forge serve, and the dash TUI — never from status.ts or ls.ts. A CLI-only operator who runs 'forge launch' then 'forge status' will see status 'running' indefinitely after the run finished (meta.status = done/failed) until they happen to open the Workbench or run 'forge wait'. This is exactly the 'lying status' / silent-failure case F0 forbids: a failed run looks alive, a done run looks in-flight.
serious.evidence: status.ts:39-53 reads `const task = store.getPlan(id); ... const meta = store.readRunMeta(id);` with no syncPlanStatus call; ls.ts:109 `let plans = store.getPlans(repoRoot).map(summarize);` likewise. Contrast wait.ts:106 `store.syncPlanStatus(t);` and serve.ts:833. core/launch.ts:6: "Status is written to ~/.forge/runs/<id>/meta.json by the bash script." store.ts:399-430 shows syncPlanStatus is the only meta→index reconciler.
serious.fix: Call store.syncPlanStatus(task) (and re-read the plan) at the top of status.ts run() and for each plan in ls.ts summarize() before printing. It is cheap (one JSON read) and already concurrency-safe via mergeRunMeta/file locks.
serious.test: Seed a plan with index status 'running' and meta.json status 'failed' in a temp FORGE dir; run forge status / forge ls and assert printed status is 'failed'.

verdict.reasoning: Traced the full path. (1) The bash runner template in src/core/launch.ts writes status transitions only to ~/.forge/runs/<id>/meta.json via set_meta_field/set_status (launch.ts:403-414; transitions at 433/455/466/473/525/592/621/701); it never touches index.json, and its forge_session_* callbacks only record sessions to SQLite. (2) store.syncPlanStatus (store.ts:399-430) is the sole meta->index reconciler; grep shows its only callers are wait.ts:106, serve.ts:833, and tui/dashboard.ts:355. (3) status.ts:39/53/58 and ls.ts:109 read the stale index via getPlan/getPlans with no sync, and cli/main.ts:109-225 has no global sync before dispatch. (4) forge launch spawns the tmux runner and exits with no watcher. No tests pin status/ls syncing. So a CLI-only operator sees index status 'running' forever after the run terminates, and 'forge ls --status done,failed' misses terminal runs entirely. Softening nuances only: 'forge status --json' includes the raw meta as 'run' (so run.status carries the truth while task.status lies), and human output shows 'tmux: [dead]' plus an 8-line log tail as indirect hints; 'forge ls' has no such hint. These mitigate but do not refute the lying-status claim.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as stated. One refinement: 'forge status --json' does embed the truthful meta.json as the 'run' field (run.status), so machine consumers can see the real state even though task.status lies; the human-readable 'status:' line and 'forge ls' (including its --status filter, which operates on the stale index value) are the genuinely lying surfaces.

serious.title: forge status human output omits errorMessage, review verdict, and quality results from the run meta
serious.detail: RunMeta carries errorMessage ('Human-readable failure reason. Surfaced on the Workbench failure card'), reviewVerdict, reviewError and qualityResults, but status.ts's human formatter only prints task fields (status/branch/agent/worktree/tmux/pr) plus a log tail. Verified live: the failed task fix-critique-trust-...-mpvgu9o4 has meta.json `"errorMessage": "Killed by user"` and per-command qualityResults, yet `bun bin/forge.ts status <id>` shows none of it — the operator gets 'status: failed' with no reason and no next action. Failure reasons are effectively Workbench-only, violating 'every terminal state must be visible in forge status'.
serious.evidence: status.ts:56-64 human lines array contains only `task.*` fields; `meta` (line 49) is used only in the JSON `result` (line 53). store.ts:97-100 defines `reviewVerdict`, `reviewError`, `errorMessage` on RunMeta. Live run: meta.json for fix-critique-trust-sidecar-result-ev-mpvgu9o4 ends with `"errorMessage": "Killed by user"` but `forge status` printed only `status: failed ... pr: (none)` + log tail.
serious.fix: In the human formatter, when meta exists print `error: <errorMessage>`, `review: <reviewVerdict|reviewError>`, and a one-line quality summary (e.g. '2/3 quality checks passed'), plus a hint line such as 'see: forge logs <id>'.
serious.test: Write meta.json with errorMessage/reviewVerdict for a failed plan in a temp store; assert forge status human output contains the error string.

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed as described, with one nuance: the failure state itself ("status: failed") is visible and the complete RunMeta (errorMessage, reviewVerdict, reviewError, qualityResults) IS emitted via `forge status --json` (status.ts:53 `result = { task, run: meta, ... }`), so machine consumers are not blind. The bug is strictly in the human formatter (status.ts:56-64), which builds lines exclusively from `task.*` fields and never touches `meta`. Verified live: meta.json for fix-critique-trust-sidecar-result-ev-mpvgu9o4 contains "errorMessage": "Killed by user" and qualityResults with `npm run test` ok:false, yet the human output shows only status/branch/agent/worktree/tmux/pr with no reason or failing-gate info. Grep confirms errorMessage/reviewVerdict/qualityResults appear in no other CLI command (only serve.ts, the Workbench), and no test pins status human output.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced the cited path end to end. (1) status.ts:49 reads RunMeta but the human formatter at lines 56-64 prints only task fields; meta flows only into the JSON result (line 53). (2) store.ts:92-100 defines qualityResults, reviewVerdict, reviewError, and errorMessage (whose doc comment explicitly says it is surfaced on the Workbench failure card — confirming Workbench-only intent). (3) Reproduced live: ran `bun bin/forge.ts status fix-critique-trust-sidecar-result-ev-mpvgu9o4 --tail 0` and got only `status: failed ... pr: (none)` while the run's meta.json ends with "errorMessage": "Killed by user" and a failed `npm run test` quality result. (4) Checked for guards elsewhere: grep over src/cli shows these fields used only in serve.ts (Workbench); no other CLI surface prints them; no tests reference cmd/status. The only mitigation is --json, which does include the meta — but the mission frame names human `forge status` as a required visibility surface for terminal states, so HIGH stands (failure reason and failing quality gate are invisible to the operator's primary CLI view, with no recovery hint).

serious.title: bun test writes fixture plans and specs into the operator's real ~/.forge (HOME redirect is a no-op under Bun)
serious.detail: tests/improve.test.ts and tests/spec-improve-error-persistence.test.ts redirect state by setting process.env.HOME to a mkdtemp dir before `new ForgeStore()`. But ForgeStore resolves its dir via os.homedir() (store.ts:201), and under Bun os.homedir() does not reflect runtime HOME mutation — verified: `bun -e 'process.env.HOME="/tmp/fakehome"; console.log(os.homedir())'` prints /Users/tcashel. package.json's test script is `bun test`, so every test run upserts fixture plans straight into the operator's production index. The operator's real ~/.forge/index.json currently contains 16+ junk plans (task-defonly-001, task-oqonly-001, ... repoRoot=/tmp/repo) and 10 task-*.md files in ~/.forge/specs; they surface in `forge spec ls` outside a repo, `forge ls --all`, and the Workbench. Since Forge's own quality gate runs the test suite, every Forge run on this repo re-pollutes operator state. Direct violation of 'all state in ~/.forge is operator data — treat as precious'.
serious.evidence: improve.test.ts:21-31 `process.env.HOME = home; ... const store = new ForgeStore();`; store.ts:201 `this.forgeDir = opts.forgeDir ?? path.join(os.homedir(), ".forge")`; runtime check under Bun returned `/Users/tcashel` despite HOME=/tmp/fakehome; real ~/.forge/index.json contains 32 plans incl. `task-defonly-001 /tmp/repo draft` etc.; package.json:20 `"test": "bun test"`.
serious.fix: Support an env override in ForgeStore (e.g. FORGE_HOME) checked before os.homedir(), and have the test helpers pass `new ForgeStore({ forgeDir })` explicitly instead of mutating HOME. Add a one-time cleanup note for the polluted entries.
serious.test: A test that constructs ForgeStore with FORGE_HOME (or forgeDir) set and asserts indexFile is NOT under os.homedir(); plus a guard test failing if any test writes to the real ~/.forge (snapshot mtime of real index.json before/after suite).

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed end to end. (1) Runtime check under Bun: os.homedir() returns /Users/tcashel even after process.env.HOME="/tmp/fakehome" — the HOME redirect is a no-op. (2) tests/improve.test.ts:21-30 and tests/spec-improve-error-persistence.test.ts:10-19 set process.env.HOME then call `new ForgeStore()` with no forgeDir; store.ts constructor resolves `opts.forgeDir ?? path.join(os.homedir(), ".forge")` and mkdirs it, and seedTask (improve.test.ts:71-72) calls store.writeSpec + store.upsertPlan, writing into the real ~/.forge. (3) The store.ts doc comment for the forgeDir option explicitly warns that HOME mutation does not redirect os.homedir() and that tests must pass forgeDir — every other test file in tests/ does pass { forgeDir }; only these two use the broken pattern. (4) Live verification: the operator's real ~/.forge/index.json contains 16 task-* junk plans whose ids exactly match the fixture ids in these two files (task-noop-001, task-oqonly-001, task-oqonly-dup-001, task-defonly-001, task-skip, task-clear, task-stable, task-clean, task-roundtrip, etc.) plus draft-* fixtures, and ~/.forge/specs contains the matching task-*.md files. The t.after cleanup only rm -rf's the empty tmp dir, so pollution persists. (5) package.json:20 is "test": "bun test", so the standard test entrypoint re-pollutes on every run. The bug is Bun-specific (Node's os.homedir() reads HOME at call time), which explains why the pattern was assumed safe. No hidden guard, no dead code, no test pinning correct behavior was found.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as stated. Minor precision: pollution corrupts/clutters operator state visible in forge ls/spec ls/Workbench but does not destroy pre-existing real plans unless a real plan id collides with a fixture id (upsertPlan would then overwrite it), so HIGH rather than BLOCKER is correct.

serious.title: --help after a positional executes the command (forge launch <id> --help would launch an agent); typo'd flags are silently swallowed everywhere
serious.detail: main.ts only honors per-command help when --help/-h is the FIRST token after the command. Every subcommand parses with parseArgs strict:false and no 'help' option, so `forge <cmd> <id> --help` silently drops --help and runs the command. Verified: `forge status <id> --help` printed the status instead of help (exit 0). For mutating commands this is a footgun: `forge launch my-task --help` resolves config and actually launches an agent run; `forge spec save --from-file x --help` saves and kicks off the improve loop. The same strict:false choice means any misspelled flag is ignored without warning — verified `forge ls --statsu running` exit 0 printed the full unfiltered list, so an operator filtering for failures can be shown a list that silently ignores their filter.
serious.evidence: main.ts:122-130 `if (rest[0] === "--help" || rest[0] === "-h") { ... }` — first-position only. launch.ts:497-518 parseArgs options have no `help` and `strict: false`. Live runs: `bun bin/forge.ts status <id> --help` → full status output, exit 0; `bun bin/forge.ts ls --statsu running` → unfiltered list, exit 0.
serious.fix: Add `help: { type: "boolean", short: "h" }` to every cmd's parseArgs and print HELP before doing work; or in main.ts scan all of rest for --help/-h before dispatch. Longer term flip to strict:true per command and convert parseArgs errors into CliError BAD_FLAG with the HELP text as hint.
serious.test: Drive main.run(["launch", "<id>", "--help"]) with a stub store and assert no launch occurs and HELP is printed; assert `ls --bogus` exits non-zero (or warns) once strict parsing lands.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code reading and live reproduction. (1) main.ts:122-130 honors per-command --help only when it is the first token after the command ('if (rest[0] === "--help" || rest[0] === "-h")'); the comment itself says "First-position only". (2) All 37 parseArgs call sites in src/cli/cmd/*.ts use strict:false and none defines a 'help' option (grep for 'strict:' and 'help' confirms), so --help after a positional lands in values unread and the command executes. (3) Live: 'bun bin/forge.ts status <id> --help' printed the full status, exit 0. (4) Live: 'bun bin/forge.ts ls --statsu running' printed the complete unfiltered list (done/draft/archived included), exit 0 — typo'd flags are silently swallowed and the intended filter is dropped. (5) launch.ts:497-518: run() has only two early exits (missing id, --dry-run); 'forge launch <id> --help' falls through to doLaunch() and would actually launch an agent (traced in code; not executed live because it is mutating). spec.ts save (line 361, strict:false, no help) has the same shape. No guard elsewhere, no test pins this. Severity HIGH stands: a help request on a mutating command performs the mutation, and a typo'd --status filter shows the operator a misleading task list (lying status).

areas.summary: The serve API surface is broadly well-shaped (typed error envelopes, validated bodies, SSE heartbeats, sentinel-based done frames), but the review surface — the operator's stated top pain — has a genuinely silent failure chain: a GitHub publish failure is only a log line (session still finalizes 'completed', no metrics/meta/UI surface), the session drawer unmounts itself on 'done' before any failure text can render, and a review/comment-fix worker that dies without finalizing leaves a permanent 'running' session that 409-blocks all future reviews of that PR with no recovery path. Dashboard-vs-reality gaps also exist outside review: dead-tmux plans stay 'Running' forever, gh failures render as an empty PR list, and a second 'forge serve' silently shares the port (verified live with three listeners on 4399 and the Workbench's repo context flapping to null). SSE itself is solid server-side but the client wrapper disables EventSource retry, wedging the review drawer on any transient drop.

minor.title: 'Improving' pill spins forever if the detached improve worker dies while serve is running — stale-critique reaper only runs at boot
minor.detail: POST /api/plans/:id/improve spawns a detached `forge spec improve` child; status surfaces via critique meta (running_critics/running_synth → 'Improving' pulse pill). reapStaleCritiques (10-min threshold) is invoked exactly once, at startServer; the periodic 60s interval only runs reapStalePlanChats. If the improve worker crashes mid-run (or the spawn'd child is killed) while serve stays up, the meta never leaves running_critics and the plan shows a pulsing 'Improving' pill indefinitely; the operator must restart forge serve to clear it.
minor.evidence: serve.ts:2707 `const swept = reapStaleCritiques(store);` — single boot-time call; serve.ts:2730-2736 the interval only calls `reapStalePlanChats(store.forgeDir)`; serve.ts:318-320 `if (critiqueMeta?.status === "running_critics" || ...) return { section: "drafting", statLabel: "Improving", statClass: "running", ... }` — pill driven purely by meta status.
minor.fix: Call reapStaleCritiques inside the existing 60s reaper interval (it already has the 10-minute staleness threshold so in-flight improves are safe), and surface the swept state as 'failed' with lastImproveError so the row lands in Attention.
minor.test: Write a critique meta with status running_critics and startedAt 11 minutes ago, run the periodic reaper tick, assert meta flips to failed and /api/plans shows the plan out of 'Improving'.

minor.title: gh failures (auth, 20s timeout, network) make /api/prs return 200 with an empty list — UI shows 'no PRs' instead of an error
minor.detail: runGh swallows all failures into {ok:false} (including the fail-fast 'configured account isn't logged in' path) and fetchPrs maps that to {prs: [], me: ''}. The /api/prs handler returns 200 ok, so the prs signal stores an empty list and clears prsError. The operator sees an empty PR view indistinguishable from 'no open PRs' while gh is actually broken — a lying dashboard on the surface that feeds the entire review flow. Same swallow applies to the per-PR 'mine' classification.
minor.evidence: gh-pr.ts:85-89 `if (resolved.error) { settle({ stdout: "", ok: false }); return; }` and :511 `if (!ok || !stdout) return { prs: [], me };` — failure collapses to empty success; serve.ts:1165-1173 returns jsonOk with whatever fetchPrs produced; prs.ts:44 `prsError.value = null;` on the 200 path so no error banner renders.
minor.fix: Make fetchPrs return a discriminated result ({ok:false, error}) and have /api/prs map it to jsonErr(502, 'GH_FAIL', ...) so prsError/banner fires; alternatively include a `warnings` array in the 200 payload and render it.
minor.test: Inject a prFetcher stub that simulates gh auth failure; assert /api/prs responds non-200 (or with warnings) and the PR view renders the error banner rather than an empty list.

minor.title: Every 3s /api/plans poll runs multiple synchronous subprocesses per plan on the event loop — server-wide stalls with many plans or slow repo mounts
minor.detail: viewTask runs per plan: isTmuxSessionAlive (execSync tmux has-session) and repoDiskInfo → detectRepo (git rev-parse via getGitRoot, detectStack/contextFile/qualityCommands fs scans, detectDefaultBranch + currentBranch git subprocesses) plus specBranchInDisk (another git rev-parse, 3s timeout). All execSync, all on Bun's single event loop, repeated on every 3-second Workbench poll and re-done a second time for the post-sync re-read. With tens of plans this is dozens-to-hundreds of subprocess spawns per tick; if any repoRoot is on a hung network volume, every other request — including SSE log pumps and POST actions — queues behind it. buildRepoViews repeats the same work for /api/repos, /api/prs, /api/config and every worktree/review route.
minor.evidence: serve.ts:454 `const repoInfo = repoDiskInfo(task.repoRoot);` inside viewTask, mapped per plan at :839; serve.ts:477 `tmuxAlive: task.tmuxSession ? isTmuxSessionAlive(task.tmuxSession) : false` (launch.ts:58-64 execSync); serve.ts:346-358 specBranchInDisk execSync with `timeout: 3000`; repo.ts:190-208 detectRepo runs getGitRoot + detectDefaultBranch + `sh("git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD", root)` per call.
minor.fix: Cache repoDiskInfo per repoRoot with a short TTL (5-10s) shared across requests; replace per-plan tmux has-session with one `tmux ls` parse per request; compute viewTask projections once, not before-and-after sync.
minor.test: Load test: 50 plans across 5 repos, measure /api/plans latency and concurrent /api/health latency during the poll; assert health stays <50ms.

minor.title: attachLogStream closes the EventSource on first error, defeating built-in retry — ReviewSessionDrawer wedges at 'running…' with no reconnect
minor.detail: EventSource fires 'error' on any transient drop (server restart, network blip) and would normally auto-reconnect; the wrapper's error listener calls handlers then src.close(), permanently killing the stream. LogTab at least exposes a manual Reconnect button, but ReviewSessionDrawer's onError only sets closeError='log stream interrupted' while terminalState stays 'running' — the drawer shows a spinner state forever, never receives 'done', and never refetches the bundle, even though the worker finishes fine in the background. The operator's only move is closing the drawer, after which completion is silent (and per the drawer-unmount bug, failure would have been silent anyway).
minor.evidence: sse.ts:57-65 `src.addEventListener("error", () => { handlers.onError?.(); handlers.onDisconnect?.(); try { src.close(); } ... })` — closes on first error; ReviewSessionDrawer.tsx:61-63 `onError: () => { setCloseError("log stream interrupted"); }` — terminalState remains 'running' (:36) and no reconnect affordance exists; LogTab.tsx:72-82 has the manual Reconnect button this drawer lacks.
minor.fix: Either let EventSource auto-retry (don't close on error; the server replays a snapshot on reconnect, so dedupe by clearing accumulated lines on snapshot) or add reconnect-with-backoff in attachLogStream plus a session-row fallback: on error, poll GET /api/agent-activity/:sessionId and treat a terminal state as done.
minor.test: Open a session log stream, kill and restart the serve process mid-stream, assert the drawer recovers and eventually receives the done event (or surfaces the session's terminal state).

minor.title: Malformed percent-encoding in API paths returns 500 INTERNAL 'URI error' instead of 400
minor.detail: Route handlers call decodeURIComponent on path captures with no guard; an invalid sequence (e.g. /api/plans/%ff) throws URIError, escapes handleApi (which has no try/catch, unlike handleApiPost), and is caught only by Bun.serve's error hook, producing a 500 INTERNAL envelope and an 'unhandled error' stderr line. Verified live: GET /api/plans/%ff → {"ok":false,"error":{"code":"INTERNAL","message":"URI error"}} and 'forge serve: unhandled error: URI error' on stderr. Cosmetic, but it pollutes logs and miscodes a client error as a server fault.
minor.evidence: serve.ts:915 `const id = decodeURIComponent(taskMatch[1]);` (same pattern at :1009, :1021, :1044, :1097, :1151, :2523 …); serve.ts:2783-2789 Bun error hook returned the observed 500. Live: `curl 'http://127.0.0.1:4399/api/plans/%ff'` → `{"ok":false,"error":{"code":"INTERNAL","message":"URI error"}}`.
minor.fix: Add a safeDecode(s) helper returning null on URIError and map null to jsonErr(400, 'BAD_PATH', ...); or wrap handleApi/handleApiDelete in the same try/catch shell handleApiPost has.
minor.test: GET /api/plans/%ff and /api/sessions/%E0/log → expect 400 BAD_PATH, no stderr 'unhandled error'.

serious.title: GitHub publish failure of review findings is completely invisible — session still ends 'completed', no surface anywhere in Workbench
serious.detail: When the operator checks 'Publish to PR' and runs a review, the worker publishes findings best-effort. If the review POST fails (gh auth, 422, network) or the publish is skipped because reconciliation of already-published ids failed, the only trace is a log line buried in the worker's agent.log. publishReviewFindings' PublishResult (posted/skippedPost) is discarded; nothing is written to session metrics, meta.json, or findings.json; the session finalizes state='completed' exitCode=0. The drawer closes on 'done' with no toast, the review bundle has no publish-status field, and the history picker shows the run as 'completed'. The operator believes findings are on GitHub when zero comments were posted — exactly the reported top pain ('PR review pipeline does NOT reliably post findings to GitHub').
serious.evidence: review-actions.ts:440-450 `await publishFindingsToGitHub({...})` — return value ignored; review-actions.ts:575-577 `catch (e) { args.log("publish to GitHub failed (best-effort): ...") }`; gh-pr-write.ts:374-376 `log('[publish] skipping review post — could not reconcile published findings...'); return { posted: 0, ..., skippedPost: true }` and :402-404 `log('[publish] review post failed...'); return { posted: 0, ... }` — both only log; review-actions.ts:459 `const status = exitCode === 0 ? "completed" : "failed"` — publish outcome never affects exitCode/metrics. No 'publish' field exists in the /api/prs/:num/review-bundle response (serve.ts:1272-1284) or ReviewRunSummary (review-actions.ts:607-617).
serious.fix: Persist the PublishResult (posted, skippedPost, error string) into the session metrics blob and meta.json in runReviewWorker; include it in ReviewRunSummary/ReviewRunDetail and the review-bundle response; in the Workbench show a red banner/toast ('Publish to GitHub failed: <err> — findings kept locally') when publishToGitHub was requested but posted==0/skippedPost. Optionally mark the session state 'completed_with_warnings'.
serious.test: Unit-test runReviewWorker with a stubbed publishReviewFindings that returns {posted:0, skippedPost:true}; assert session metrics record publishFailed and that GET /api/prs/:num/reviews surfaces it. UI test: drawer/toast renders the publish-failure banner.

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed end to end, with one refinement: local findings are NOT lost (findings.json is written at review-actions.ts:434 before publish and renders in the Workbench as forgeFindings), so the invisible artifact is specifically the publish OUTCOME, not the findings. The failure trace: publishReviewFindings (gh-pr-write.ts:365-410) never throws for post failure or reconciliation skip — it returns a PublishResult after only log() calls (375-376, 402-404). Its sole production caller, publishFindingsToGitHub (review-actions.ts:544-578), discards that result (line 564) and additionally swallows any thrown error at 575-577. The worker's outer catch therefore never fires; exitCode stays 0, finalizeSession writes state='completed' error=null (473-480), meta.json status='completed' (459-471), and no publish field exists in metrics, the review-bundle response (serve.ts:1272-1284), ReviewRunSummary (review-actions.ts:607-617), or ReviewRunDetail. The drawer evidence is stronger than claimed: ReviewSessionDrawer.tsx onDone (54-59) nulls activeReviewSession and line 90 returns null, so the drawer UNMOUNTS in the same render as 'done' — even the live-streamed '[publish] review post failed' line disappears instantly; there is no toast component. Tests (tests/core/gh-pr-write.test.ts, tests/review-actions.test.ts) pin the PublishResult values and the publish gating but nothing pins any surfacing of publish failure. The only persistent trace is the agent.log file in the run dir.
verdict.reasoning: I re-traced every cited line and they all check out exactly as quoted: (1) review-actions.ts:441/564 discard the PublishResult; (2) gh-pr-write.ts:374-376 and 402-404 convert reconciliation failure and POST failure into log-only return values; (3) review-actions.ts:459 derives status purely from the agent exitCode, which publish cannot affect because publishFindingsToGitHub catches everything at 575-577; (4) serve.ts review-bundle (1272-1284) and the reviews list/detail endpoints expose no publish status; (5) ReviewSessionDrawer.tsx:54-59 + 90 unmount the drawer on 'done', destroying the live log including the failure line. I searched for compensating guards: grep shows publishReviewFindings has exactly one production caller (the worker); no other code reads skippedPost/posted; tests pin only the return shape and the checkbox gating (shouldPublishToGitHub), not any surfacing. No toast system, no metrics field, no meta.json field carries publish state. The one nuance the claim slightly overstates is 'findings invisible' — findings remain visible locally as forgeFindings in the bundle; it is the publish failure that is invisible, which the claim's title actually states correctly. Per the audit's severity lens this is a silent failure of a core flow and matches the operator's documented top pain, so BLOCKER stands.

serious.title: ReviewSessionDrawer clears the active-session signal inside onDone, unmounting itself before the failure/error message can render
serious.detail: The drawer's onDone handler sets terminalState ('done'/'failed') and closeError, then immediately sets activeReviewSession/activeCommentFixSession to null. The component computes `open` from those signals and returns null when they're cleared, so the drawer unmounts on the very next render — the 'failed' state badge and the worker error text (e.g. 'no fenced forge-review block in reviewer output', 'reviewer agent exited non-zero') are never visible. A failed ad-hoc review or comment-fix therefore presents as: drawer silently vanishes, no toast, findings unchanged. The run-history list isn't refetched either (only loadReviewBundle is called), so the failed run doesn't even appear in 'Past Forge reviews' until the page remounts.
serious.evidence: ReviewSessionDrawer.tsx:54-60 `onDone: (e) => { setTerminalState(e.exitCode === 0 ? "done" : "failed"); setCloseError(e.error); void loadReviewBundle(...); if (active.kind === "comment-fix") activeCommentFixSession.value = null; else activeReviewSession.value = null; }`; :34 `const open = active !== null && active.prNum === prNumber;`; :90 `if (!open || !active) return null;` — nulling the signal re-renders with active=null and the drawer (including the closeError div at :107) disappears.
serious.fix: Keep the drawer mounted after done: track the session locally (copy sessionId/kind into component state when it becomes active) and only clear the global signal when the user closes the drawer; on done also call loadReviewRuns. Alternatively fire showToast('Forge review failed: <error>', 'error') on non-zero exit before clearing the signal.
serious.test: Component test: mount drawer with an active session, fire the SSE 'done' handler with exitCode=1/error text, assert the drawer is still in the DOM showing state 'failed' and the error string.

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed as described, with one minor mitigation for the comment-fix sub-case. Trace: serve.ts logSseResponse emits `done` with `{exitCode, error}` from the sessions row (src/cli/cmd/serve.ts:643-647) or the sentinel; sse.ts attachLogStream forwards it to onDone (src/web/lib/sse.ts:41-50). ReviewSessionDrawer.tsx:54-60 sets terminalState/closeError then immediately nulls activeReviewSession/activeCommentFixSession; lines 34 and 90 (`const open = active !== null && ...` / `if (!open || !active) return null;`) make the component unmount on the very next render, so the "failed" badge (line 100) and closeError div (line 107) never paint. No other live surface exists: ReviewActionBar.tsx only shows startError for failures to START a review (lines 28-33); PrReviewBundle (src/web/types.ts:408-420) has no run-error field, so the loadReviewBundle refetch shows nothing for a failed ad-hoc review; loadReviewRuns is called only from ReviewPage.tsx:43 in a useEffect keyed on [num, repo], so the run-history list ("Past Forge reviews") is not refetched on done — confirmed by grep, no other caller. No tests reference ReviewSessionDrawer. Minor mitigation: for comment-fix sessions only, per-target `failed` entries in bundle.commentFixState do surface in FindingsRail.tsx:81 / CommentThread.tsx:44 after the refetch — but only if the worker progressed far enough to write them; a worker that crashes early (agent exited non-zero, no fenced block) is still fully invisible. Ad-hoc review failures are invisible in all cases until page remount/PR switch. Severity HIGH (not BLOCKER) is right: the failed state IS persisted in the sessions table and shows in review history after a remount, so nothing is lost — but live status lies (drawer vanishes as if nothing happened) in the operator's stated top-pain pipeline.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced the full path myself. (1) Drawer code at /Users/tcashel/repositories/forge/src/web/components/review/ReviewSessionDrawer.tsx:54-60 matches the quoted evidence exactly: onDone sets state then nulls the signal; lines 34/90 compute `open` from that signal and return null, unmounting before the failure UI can render. (2) Checked for guards/alternate surfaces the auditor might have missed: ReviewActionBar only surfaces start-time errors; the review-bundle refetch carries no run error (PrReviewBundle has no such field); reviewRunsError only covers history-fetch failures; loadReviewRuns has exactly one caller (ReviewPage useEffect on [num, repo]), so the history list is indeed stale after done. (3) Confirmed the lost data is real: serve.ts:636-651 emits the `done` frame with the sessions row's error text (e.g. set from review-actions.ts:416/429 error strings), and sse.ts:41-50 delivers it to the drawer, which is the only consumer. (4) No tests pin this component. Only correction: comment-fix per-target failures can partially surface via bundle.commentFixState badges when the worker recorded them, which slightly narrows but does not refute the claim.

serious.title: Review/comment-fix sessions killed without finalizing stay 'running' forever — REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT/FIX_IN_FLIGHT 409 blocks all future reviews of that PR with no recovery path
serious.detail: The detached __review-worker / comment-fix worker finalizes its sessions row on exit, but if the worker dies without running that code (SIGKILL, machine reboot, bun crash, power loss mid-run) the row stays state='running' permanently. There is no reconciliation for purpose='review' or 'comment-fix': reconcileExecutionSessions only covers purpose='execution', reconcileCritiqueSessions only 'critique'/'synthesis'. Consequences: (a) the single-flight guard rejects every new review/fix for that (repo, PR) with 409 REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT/FIX_IN_FLIGHT forever; (b) the Run Forge review button stays disabled ('Forge review running…'); (c) the drawer and Agent Activity show 'running' indefinitely (no sentinel ever appears, and emitDoneFromSession polls a row that never goes terminal). There is no kill endpoint for sessions and no CLI escape hatch surfaced in the UI — the review pipeline for that PR is unrecoverably wedged until the operator hand-edits SQLite.
serious.evidence: review-actions.ts:170-186 in-flight guard: `SELECT id FROM sessions WHERE purpose = 'review' AND state = 'running' AND json_extract(metrics,'$.prNum') = ? ...` → throws REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT; comment-fix-actions.ts:304-320 same with FIX_IN_FLIGHT; db/writes.ts:212-224 `WHERE sessions.purpose = 'execution' AND sessions.state = 'running'` — only execution; critique.ts:613-615 `WHERE s.purpose IN ('critique','synthesis')`. No code path anywhere flips a dead 'review'/'comment-fix' session out of 'running' (grep over src/core, src/cli).
serious.fix: Add a reaper (boot + the existing 60s interval in startServer) that marks review/comment-fix sessions failed when stale — e.g. store the worker pid in metrics at spawn and check process liveness, or treat rows with started_at older than a max-runtime threshold and no recent agent.log mtime as dead. Also let the in-flight guard self-heal by ignoring rows whose pid is dead.
serious.test: Insert a sessions row purpose='review' state='running' with started_at 2h ago and a dead pid; boot startServer (or call the reaper) and assert the row flips to failed and POST /api/prs/:num/run-review succeeds.

verdict.correctedDetail: Core claim confirmed; two peripheral details are overstated. (1) The "Run Forge review" button does not stay disabled forever: activeReviewSession is a client-only signal (src/web/signals/review.ts:24, set only in ReviewActionBar.tsx:27), so after a page reload or drawer close the button re-enables — but every click then surfaces the 409 REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT error, so the pipeline is still wedged, just not silently. (2) "Hand-edit SQLite" is not strictly the only escape: `forge session finish --id <sid> --exit-code 1` (src/cli/cmd/session.ts:102-148, dispatched via main.ts:189) would finalize the stuck row without SQL — but it is documented only as a bash-runner-internal hook, and the REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT/FIX_IN_FLIGHT CliErrors carry no hint pointing to it, so in practice there is no discoverable recovery path.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced every cited path; the wedge is real. (a) In-flight guards: review-actions.ts:170-186 selects `purpose='review' AND state='running' AND json_extract(metrics,'$.prNum')=?` with no time bound or liveness check and throws REVIEW_IN_FLIGHT; comment-fix-actions.ts:304-320 is identical with FIX_IN_FLIGHT. serve.ts:2311/2366 map these to HTTP 409. (b) The workers are fully detached: review-actions.ts:246-254 `Bun.spawn({cmd:[bun, script, "__review-worker", sessionId]...}); proc.unref();` (comment-fix-actions.ts:390-396 same) — no pid is recorded and the parent never observes exit; the only finalizeSession calls for these purposes are inside the worker itself or the spawn-failure rollback (review-actions.ts:264,326,473; comment-fix-actions.ts:404,465,788). SIGKILL/reboot mid-run leaves state='running' forever. (c) No reconciliation covers these purposes: writes.ts:212-224 reconcileExecutionSessions is `WHERE sessions.purpose='execution' ... AND j.session_id = sessions.id` (joined to jobs — review sessions have no job row, so it could never match them anyway); critique.ts:609-614 reconcileCritiqueSessions is `WHERE s.purpose IN ('critique','synthesis')`; the only boot sweeps in serve.ts are reapStaleCritiques (serve.ts:2672-2689, critique meta files) and reapStalePlanChats (serve.ts:2713) — neither touches review/comment-fix session rows. (d) The SSE done path is read-only: serve.ts:636-656 emitDoneFromSession just polls `SELECT state... FROM sessions` and emits `done` only on completed/failed/killed — it never flips the row, and the sentinel (serve.ts:622) only appears if the worker wrote its last line. listForgeReviews (review-actions.ts:701-738) reads row.state directly, so history shows 'in progress' forever and ReviewHistoryPicker.tsx:67 refuses to open running rows. (e) The only kill endpoint is plan-scoped (serve.ts:1906 `/api/plans/:id/(launch|...|kill...)`) — nothing for sessions. Tests (tests/sessions.test.ts:184) pin only execution reconciliation. So a hard-killed review/comment-fix worker permanently 409-blocks all future reviews/fixes for that (repo, PR) with no automatic or surfaced recovery — HIGH is the right severity (unrecoverable state in the operator's top-pain pipeline; not BLOCKER since the 409 error is visible and no findings are silently dropped).

serious.title: Plan stuck in 'Running' forever when the tmux runner dies without writing terminal meta — dashboard lies about a dead job
serious.detail: The /api/plans poll syncs running plans via store.syncPlanStatus, which only reads the runner's meta.json status field and returns early when it hasn't changed. If the tmux session/runner dies without writing a terminal status (tmux server killed, reboot, OOM), the plan stays status='running' and renders in the Running section with a pulsing 'Running' pill indefinitely. The server even computes the truth — viewTask sets tmuxAlive=false via isTmuxSessionAlive — but the UI uses tmuxAlive only for a small '● tmux alive' dot and an 'Attach tmux' button in the detail head; the list row, section, and 'forge status'-equivalent never reconcile. Operator-visible consequence: a dead overnight run looks alive; no failure ever surfaces ('no silent failure' violated).
serious.evidence: serve.ts:826-835 sync loop calls `store.syncPlanStatus(t)` for running-family plans only; store.ts:400-408 `const newStatus = meta.status...; if (!newStatus || newStatus === plan.status) return null;` — meta is the only signal, dead tmux never flips status; serve.ts:477 `tmuxAlive: task.tmuxSession ? isTmuxSessionAlive(task.tmuxSession) : false` is computed but DetailHead.tsx:172 only renders it as a passive dot (`{t.tmuxAlive ? ● tmux alive : null}`).
serious.fix: In the /api/plans sync loop (or a periodic reaper), when status is running-family AND tmuxSession is set AND !isTmuxSessionAlive AND the log file hasn't grown for a grace period (e.g. 60s after launch), merge run meta {status:'failed', errorMessage:'runner died (tmux session gone)'} and syncJobState — same as the kill path at serve.ts:2549-2566.
serious.test: Create a plan with status running and a bogus tmuxSession name plus a stale log; hit GET /api/plans twice and assert the plan transitions to section 'attention' with a 'runner died' error.

verdict.reasoning: Re-traced end to end. syncPlanStatus (store.ts:400-408) is purely meta.json-driven with no tmux-liveness fallback; the bash runner script in launch.ts has zero `trap` handlers, so any abnormal death (tmux server kill, reboot, OOM) leaves meta.status='running' permanently. No reaper covers plans: reapStaleCritiques is critique-meta only, reapStalePlanChats is plan-chat only, and reconcileExecutionSessions (db/writes.ts:212) requires the job row to already be terminal — exactly the state that never gets written in this failure mode. statusInfo (serve.ts:271) maps running→"Running" pill unconditionally, and on the web side tmuxAlive is consumed only by DetailHead.tsx (alive-dot ternary rendering null when dead, plus gating the Attach button); PlanRow.tsx never reads it. So a dead overnight run sits in the Workbench Running section pulsing forever — confirmed silent-failure / lying-status, HIGH per the severity lens. One overstatement in the claim: CLI surfaces are not fully blind — `forge status` prints "[dead]" (status.ts:62), the TUI dashboard renders "dead" (dashboard.ts:69-71), and `forge wait` exits 3 STALLED on dead tmux (wait.ts:122-128). But these are secondary annotations the operator must actively seek; the status field never reconciles anywhere, and the Workbench (the named surface) shows no indication at all.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate except for "'forge status'-equivalent never reconcile": forge status shows "[dead]" on the tmux line (src/cli/cmd/status.ts:62), the TUI dashboard shows "dead" (src/tui/dashboard.ts:69-71), and forge wait detects the condition and exits STALLED (src/cli/cmd/wait.ts:122-128). The real bug is narrower but still HIGH: plan.status itself never auto-transitions to failed on dead tmux (no trap in the launch.ts runner script, no plan-level reaper, syncPlanStatus is meta-only), and the Workbench — list row (PlanRow.tsx ignores tmuxAlive), section assignment (statusInfo serve.ts:271), and detail head (DetailHead.tsx:172 renders null when dead) — gives zero dead-run indication, so a dead job pulses as "Running" indefinitely in the UI.

serious.title: Second 'forge serve' on an occupied port binds successfully (SO_REUSEPORT) — two servers silently share the port with divergent state
serious.detail: Bun.serve does not fail when the port is already taken by another forge serve. Verified live: three bun processes simultaneously LISTENing on 127.0.0.1:4399 (lsof), each 'forge serve' reporting success. Worse, on macOS the newest binder captures the traffic: after starting a second serve from /tmp, GET /api/workbench/context flipped from currentRepo=forge to currentRepo=null for the existing Workbench tab — the dashboard silently changed identity. Cross-process hazards follow: plan-chat abortInFlight is an in-process map so an abort POST can land on the wrong process; each boot also `fs.rmSync`s and rebuilds the shared src/web/dist the other process is serving. An operator who absent-mindedly runs serve twice gets a flapping, self-inconsistent Workbench with zero error anywhere.
serious.evidence: serve.ts:2779-2837 `Bun.serve({ port, hostname: host, ... })` with no bind-conflict handling; serve.ts:2740-2743 `fs.rmSync(webDistDir, { recursive: true, force: true })` on every boot. Live repro: `lsof -nP -iTCP:4399 -sTCP:LISTEN` showed PIDs 87049/96906/97047 all LISTEN on 127.0.0.1:4399; `curl /api/workbench/context` returned `"currentRepo":null` (served by the /tmp instance) while the original instance returns the forge repo — after killing the extra instances it returned `"currentRepo":{"name":"forge",...}`.
serious.fix: Before Bun.serve, probe the port with an exclusive bind (or GET /api/health) and exit with CliError PORT_IN_USE ('forge serve already running at http://127.0.0.1:7456'); ensure reusePort is not enabled. Also write a pidfile under ~/.forge to detect an existing instance.
serious.test: startServer twice on the same port in a test; assert the second call rejects with PORT_IN_USE instead of resolving.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by code reading and independent live reproduction. serve.ts:2779-2837 calls Bun.serve({ port, hostname: host, development: false, ... }) with no bind-conflict handling, and there is no guard anywhere else (no EADDRINUSE handling, no lock file, no pre-bind check; run() at serve.ts:2867-2914 just calls startServer). I reproduced the double-bind independently on this machine (macOS, Bun 1.3.13): two minimal Bun.serve processes with the same options both bound 127.0.0.1:4398 without error, lsof showed both PIDs LISTENing, and all requests were answered by the newest binder — confirming silent port sharing with last-binder-wins traffic capture. The divergent-state consequences are also real: currentRepo is computed once from detectRepo(process.cwd()) at serve.ts:2770-2774 (a serve from /tmp yields currentRepo=null, flipping the Workbench identity); abortInFlight is an in-process Map (src/core/plan-chat.ts:301, 311-321), so an abort POST routed to the wrong process is a silent no-op while the real subprocess survives; and serve.ts:2741-2743 rmSync's the shared src/web/dist on every boot. This violates the F0 'no silent failure' rule — the second serve reports success and the operator gets a flapping, self-inconsistent Workbench with no error. HIGH is the correct severity (lying status / silent cross-process inconsistency, not data loss). Only nuance: the double-bind is macOS SO_REUSEPORT semantics and would likely fail loudly on Linux, but the operator's environment is darwin, so the bug stands as stated.

serious.title: improve.test.ts and spec-improve-error-persistence.test.ts write into the operator's real ~/.forge (broken HOME isolation under Bun)
serious.detail: withTmpHome() sets process.env.HOME mid-run and then constructs `new ForgeStore()` with no forgeDir. Under Bun, os.homedir() does NOT pick up mid-run HOME changes (verified: `bun -e` returned /Users/tcashel after setting HOME=/tmp/fake-home-test), so the store resolves to the REAL ~/.forge. Every `bun test` run silently writes test plans into the operator's production index.json, specs/, and forge.db. Verified pollution: forge.db plans table contains task-app-001, task-cum-001, task-oq-001, task-oq-dedupe-empty-001, task-oqonly-001 created at 2026-06-09T23:57 (today's test run); ~/.forge/index.json contains task-skip, task-clear, task-noop, task-clean, task-stable, task-roundtrip; ~/.forge/specs/ has 10+ task-*.md files. Consequence: garbage plans appear in `forge ls`/`forge status`/Workbench (lying status), and a test id colliding with a real plan would overwrite operator data. serve.test.ts:36-37 even documents the trap: "os.homedir() is captured at process start and won't pick up mid-run HOME tweaks, so we can't isolate via env."
serious.evidence: tests/improve.test.ts:23-30: `process.env.HOME = home; ... const store = new ForgeStore();` — no forgeDir override. src/core/store.ts:201: `this.forgeDir = opts.forgeDir ?? path.join(os.homedir(), ".forge")`. Verified live: `sqlite3 ~/.forge/forge.db "select id, created_at from plans where id like 'task-%'"` → `task-app-001|2026-06-09T23:57:17.833Z` (+4 more rows); tests/improve.test.ts:282/309/347/375 seed exactly these ids (`seedTask(store, "task-app-001", ...)`). Bun homedir check: `bun -e '...process.env.HOME="/tmp/fake-home-test"; console.log(os.homedir())'` → `/Users/tcashel`.
serious.fix: Replace the HOME-swap pattern with the explicit constructor used by serve.test.ts:38: `new ForgeStore({ forgeDir: path.join(tmpHome, ".forge") })` in both files; delete the env mutation. Then purge the polluted rows/files from the operator's ~/.forge.
serious.test: Suite-level guard: snapshot fs.statSync(os.homedir()+"/.forge/forge.db").mtimeMs in a globalSetup and assert it is unchanged in globalTeardown; unit test asserting withTmpHome's store.forgeDir startsWith(os.tmpdir()).

verdict.reasoning: Every link in the chain was independently re-verified. (1) tests/improve.test.ts:21-31 and tests/spec-improve-error-persistence.test.ts:10-20 both mutate process.env.HOME mid-run and then construct `new ForgeStore()` with no forgeDir override. (2) src/core/store.ts:201 resolves `opts.forgeDir ?? path.join(os.homedir(), ".forge")` and mkdirSyncs into it in the constructor. (3) Reproduced under Bun: `os.homedir()` returned /Users/tcashel both before and after setting process.env.HOME, and package.json:20 confirms `"test": "bun test"`, so the stores in these tests resolve to the REAL ~/.forge. (4) The codebase documents this exact trap in src/core/store.ts:181-184 and tests/serve.test.ts:36-37, where serve.test.ts correctly passes an explicit forgeDir — these two test files ignore that contract; no hidden guard exists. (5) Pollution confirmed live: ~/.forge/specs/ has 10 task-*.md files matching seedTask ids at improve.test.ts:309/375/503/535/579; ~/.forge/index.json contains all 16 test ids including task-skip/task-clear/task-noop/task-clean/task-stable/task-roundtrip from spec-improve-error-persistence.test.ts; sqlite3 ~/.forge/forge.db shows 5 task-* plan rows created 2026-06-09T23:57. The t.after cleanup only removes the empty tmp dir, so pollution persists silently after every `bun test` run.
verdict.correctedDetail: Claim is accurate as written. Only severity needs adjustment: the demonstrated harm is persistent garbage plans in the operator's production ~/.forge (index.json, specs/, forge.db) surfacing in forge ls/status/Workbench — lying status. Actual data loss (the BLOCKER criterion) is conditional on a real plan id colliding with a task-* test id; upsertPlan/writeSpec do overwrite by id unconditionally so the destruction path exists, but no operator data was actually destroyed.

serious.title: Nondeterministic HTTP-test wedge cascades into 'test() inside another test()' errors that silently skip entire test files
serious.detail: The suite is wildly nondeterministic: three consecutive `bun test` runs executed 431 tests (8 fail), 222 tests (36 fail), and 304 tests (28 fail, 19 errors). When one in-process HTTP test wedges past its timeout (run 2: 'GET /api/jobs/:id returns 404 UNKNOWN_JOB' at 63790ms; run 3: 'POST /api/specs/:id/plan-chat rejects empty message' at 154308ms with 'timed out after 5000ms, before its done callback was called' — Bun's shim misreads the `(t)` context param as a done callback), Bun's node:test shim then throws NotImplementedError 'test() inside another test() is not yet implemented in Bun (issue #5090)' for every subsequent test() registration, aborting whole files (store-atomic, agent-command, review, critique-runner-rescue, ...). The run still ends with a normal-looking pass/fail tally, so ~50% of the suite is silently not executed — the quality gate lies, directly violating F0 'no silent failure' and 'suite health: flakes eliminated'.
serious.evidence: /tmp/forge-test-run3.txt:247-248: `(fail) POST /api/specs/:id/plan-chat rejects empty message [154308.39ms]` + `^ this test timed out after 5000ms, before its done callback was called`; :264: `NotImplementedError: test() inside another test() is not yet implemented in Bun ... Use bun:test in the interim` repeated 19 times (lines 255-531) against store-atomic.test.ts:27, agent-command.test.ts:42, review.test.ts, critique-runner-rescue.test.ts:134. Totals: run1 `423 pass / 8 fail / Ran 431 tests`; run2 `186 pass / 36 fail / Ran 222 tests`; run3 `276 pass / 28 fail / 19 errors / Ran 304 tests across 46 files`. The wedged test itself is trivial (tests/plan-chat.test.ts:365-372 posts `{}` and expects 400).
serious.fix: Migrate tests from `node:test` to `bun:test` (the shim's own error message recommends it), which removes both the done-callback misinterpretation and the nested-test cascade; separately hunt the wedge source (in-process fetch against Bun.serve plus detached children from the improve test — see docs-suite-serve-test-spawns-real-cli).
serious.test: CI job that runs `bun test` twice and fails if the 'Ran N tests' total differs between runs or if 'NotImplementedError' / 'Unhandled error between tests' appears in output.

verdict.correctedDetail: Kernel of truth, heavily overstated. Real residual issue: the suite uses `node:test` under Bun (tests/plan-chat.test.ts:18, tests/serve.test.ts:14, etc.), so if any single test hangs past its timeout while its promise stays pending, Bun's shim throws NotImplementedError 'test() inside another test()' (Bun #5090) for every subsequently loaded file, aborting their registration — this genuinely happened once, evidenced by /tmp/forge-test-run3.txt (19 NotImplementedError blocks, 304 of 431 tests ran). However: (1) it is NOT silent — the tally prints '19 errors', each aborted file prints a named error block, and bun exits nonzero; (2) it is NOT reproducible — 5 sequential runs were byte-identical (423 pass / 8 fail / Ran 431 tests, ~27s, zero NIE) and even 3 concurrent suites all ran 431 tests; the auditor's wedge runs took 54-178s vs 27s baseline, i.e. captured under heavy external load; (3) the auditor's run2 evidence (186/36/222, UNKNOWN_JOB wedge at 63790ms) contradicts the actual on-disk /tmp/forge-test-run2.txt (422/9/431, no NIE); (4) the '(t) misread as done callback' mechanism is wrong — async (t) tests pass routinely; (5) the gate is already red on main every run (8 deterministic worktrees.test.ts failures), so this cannot turn a red gate green. Corrected finding: fragile node:test-on-Bun harness where one hung test can abort later files' registration (loud, not silent), plus mild load flakiness (improve.test.ts) — migrate tests to bun:test to remove the failure mode.
verdict.reasoning: Re-traced everything. Confirmed: cited files import node:test (plan-chat.test.ts:18, serve.test.ts:14, store-atomic.test.ts:6, agent-command.test.ts:6, critique-runner-rescue.test.ts:29) on Bun 1.3.13, and /tmp/forge-test-run3.txt does contain the cascade exactly as quoted (line 264 etc., 19 NotImplementedError, 276/28/19err/304-of-46-files). Refuted: (a) 'wildly nondeterministic' — my 5 sequential `bun test` runs were identical (423/8/431, ~27s, 0 NIE) and 3 concurrent runs still each ran all 431 tests; the cascade never reproduced in 8 attempts; (b) the auditor's run2 citation is false — /tmp/forge-test-run2.txt actually shows 422 pass / 9 fail / Ran 431 tests with no 63790ms wedge and no NIE; (c) 'silently not executed / normal-looking tally' — the run3 tally explicitly says '19 errors', each aborted file prints a named NotImplementedError, and exit code is nonzero, so any exit-code gate fails loudly; (d) the shim does not misread (t) as a done callback — all async (t) tests pass in normal runs; (e) the suite already fails 8 worktrees.test.ts tests deterministically on main, so the gate is red regardless. The real, evidenced residue is a fragile node:test-on-Bun harness (one rare hung test under heavy load aborts later file registration) plus mild concurrency flakiness — MEDIUM per the lens ('flaky test', friction), not HIGH 'silent failure'.

serious.title: Confirmed: worktrees.test.ts makes real git commits inheriting the user's gpg-signing config — 8 deterministic failures, hang risk with an interactive signer
serious.detail: All three runs reproduce 8 failures in tests/worktrees.test.ts: the git() helper shells out to the real git binary with the user's global config, so `git commit` attempts gpg signing and fails 'gpg: signing failed: No secret key' for the synthetic test identity. On a machine where the signer prompts (the canonical 1Password gpgsign trap in this repo's own memory), execFileSync would block indefinitely — a headless hang, not just a failure. This makes the suite red on any operator machine with commit.gpgsign=true, masking real regressions in the worktree subsystem.
serious.evidence: tests/worktrees.test.ts:51-52: `function git(repoRoot, args) { return execFileSync("git", ["-C", repoRoot, ...args], ...) }` — no GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL isolation, no `-c commit.gpgsign=false`. Run output: `error: gpg failed to sign the data ... gpg: skipped "Test <test@example.com>": No secret key ... fatal: failed to write commit object` at worktrees.test.ts:239, 268, 320, 356, 389, 431, 558, 594 (8 tests, identical across runs 1-3).
serious.fix: In git()/gitRaw(), pass `env: { ...process.env, GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL: "/dev/null", GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM: "/dev/null", GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM: "1" }` and set user.name/user.email via `-c` flags (or `git config` in the temp repo), plus `-c commit.gpgsign=false`.
serious.test: Run the worktrees suite with a global gitconfig containing `commit.gpgsign=true` pointed at a nonexistent key and assert all tests pass.

verdict.correctedDetail: Confirmed and reproduced: tests/worktrees.test.ts:51-53 git() helper shells out to real git with the inherited user environment; no GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM isolation or `commit.gpgsign=false` exists anywhere in tests/ or src/ (grep returns zero matches). Tests set only local user.email/user.name. On this machine (global commit.gpgsign=true, no key for 'Test <test@example.com>') `bun test tests/worktrees.test.ts` yields 17 pass / 8 fail with 'gpg: signing failed: No secret key ... fatal: failed to write commit object' at the cited lines (239, 268, 320, 356, 389, 431, 558, 594). One correction: with the current openpgp config the failure is fast and deterministic — no hang occurs; the indefinite-block scenario is conditional on a prompting signer (ssh/1Password format or pinentry with a real key) and was not observed. Net effect: all git-backed worktree integration tests are deterministically red on any operator machine with commit.gpgsign=true, masking real regressions in src/core/worktrees.ts, while passing in unsigned CI environments.
verdict.reasoning: Refutation attempt failed — the bug is real. I verified there is no hidden guard: grep for GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/gpgsign/GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM across tests/ and src/ finds nothing; package.json test script is plain `bun test` with no env isolation; the test repos configure only user.email/user.name locally. I reproduced the exact failure live: 8/25 tests fail with 'gpg: skipped "Test <test@example.com>": No secret key' through git() at worktrees.test.ts:52, matching every cited line. The code is not dead and no test pins around it. Severity adjusted HIGH -> MEDIUM per the audit's own lens: this is a test-isolation defect (deterministic red suite on signing machines, masking worktree regressions), not a production crash/hang/data-loss; the claimed indefinite hang is configuration-dependent (would require a prompting signer such as 1Password ssh signing) and does not occur with the machine's actual config, which fails fast. Fix is trivial: set GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL=/dev/null (or `-c commit.gpgsign=false`) in the git()/gitRaw() helpers.

serious.title: serve.test.ts improve-queue test spawns a real detached `forge spec improve` child that operates on the real ~/.forge and outlives the test
serious.detail: The test 'POST /api/plans/:id/improve queues a draft task' exercises the real spawn path: serve.ts spawns a detached, unref'd `bun bin/forge.ts spec improve <id> --json` with `env: process.env`. The child process re-resolves ~/.forge from os.homedir() (no FORGE_HOME mechanism exists), so it looks up the plan in the operator's REAL store, not the test's tmp forgeDir. Today it fails fast because 'improve-queue' doesn't exist in the real store — but combined with the HOME-pollution finding, a colliding id would launch real critic/synthesizer agent runs (claude/codex) headless from a unit-test run, and on failure write lastImproveError into the real DB. The orphaned detached children are also prime suspects for the suite wedge (finding docs-suite-wedged-test-...).
serious.evidence: tests/serve.test.ts:1145-1158: posts to `/api/plans/improve-queue/improve`, asserts `body.data!.queued === true` and `typeof body.data!.pid === "number"` — a real child was spawned. src/cli/cmd/serve.ts:2632-2634: `const child = spawnForgeCli(["spec", "improve", planId, "--json"], { cwd: task.repoRoot, env: process.env })`. cli-spawn.ts spawns detached + unref. src/core/store.ts:201: child resolves `path.join(os.homedir(), ".forge")` — the test's `ForgeStore({ forgeDir })` override (serve.test.ts:38) does not propagate to the child.
serious.fix: Introduce a FORGE_HOME env override honored by ForgeStore (`opts.forgeDir ?? process.env.FORGE_HOME ?? homedir()/.forge`), have startServer pass it through to spawnForgeCli, and set it in test bootServer(); or inject a stub spawner via ServeOptions for tests.
serious.test: Boot the test server with a tmp forgeDir, hit /improve, wait for the child to exit, and assert the child wrote its failure record into the tmp forgeDir (not the real one) — e.g. by pre-seeding the plan in the tmp store only.

verdict.reasoning: The claim's mechanism is provably wrong. spawnForgeCli (src/core/cli-spawn.ts:47,61-62) spawns `[Bun.which("bun"), process.argv[1], ...args]`. Under `bun test`, process.argv[1] is the currently-executing TEST FILE, not bin/forge.ts (empirically verified: argv = ["/Users/tcashel/.bun/bin/bun", "<test file path>"] for both `bun test` and `bun test <file>`). So the child spawned by the 'queues a draft task' test (tests/serve.test.ts:1145-1158 via src/cli/cmd/serve.ts:2632) is `bun tests/serve.test.ts spec improve improve-queue --json`. I ran that exact command: it crashes during module evaluation at the first test() call with "error: Cannot use test outside of the test runner. Run \"bun test\"" (node:test:144), before any forge code executes. All os.homedir() resolutions are inside constructors (src/core/store.ts:201, src/core/db/connection.ts:34), none at module scope, so the child's import phase touches nothing. Consequently the child NEVER resolves the real ~/.forge, never reads the operator's store, cannot launch critic/synthesizer agents on an id collision, cannot write lastImproveError to the real DB, and exits in milliseconds — it also cannot be the suite-wedge suspect. Even the claim's stated current behavior ("fails fast because improve-queue doesn't exist in the real store") is wrong: it fails fast because the spawned script is the test file. The only true fragment is that the test spawns a real detached child via an unmocked path, but that child is inert.
verdict.correctedDetail: The real (much smaller) issue: the improve-queue test exercises spawnForgeCli unmocked, but because process.argv[1] under `bun test` is the test file rather than bin/forge.ts, the spawned child is `bun tests/serve.test.ts spec improve improve-queue --json`, which instantly errors with "Cannot use test outside of the test runner". The test's assertions (queued===true, typeof pid==="number") therefore pass vacuously — the test never validates that `forge spec improve` actually launches in production, and spawnForgeCli's argv[1]-based script resolution is untestable under the bun test runner. Test-fidelity gap, not a real-store-contamination or headless-safety bug.
